What Does It Mean to Invest In The “Sustainability” Of Something?
Sustainability is not an easy topic to discuss. But, it is one that continues to become even more important in the eyes of many — in both business and personal contexts.
The Sustainability Alliance defines “sustainability” as “[…] the ability to continue in existence and thrive, making sure that one generation has what it needs to thrive as well.” This means that it doesn’t necessarily require an end to what it “gives” to future generations.
So, this doesn’t give room – in either the personal or business sector – for investing in sustainability as much as it encourages us to recognize a need for it and to take active steps toward it.
How Do People Feel About Investing In Sustainability?
This question was asked on Quora, a question-and-answer website with over 1 billion monthly queries. The following chart shows a broad overview of answers received from respondents.
Some of the most common answers given included the following.

The Brick Store Museum is a historic brick store, owned by the City of Warrenton, North Carolina. Built in the 1840s, and listed in the National Register of Historic Places, The Brick Store is Warrenton's oldest commercial building. It hosts a collection of early Warrenton history which reflects over 150 years in this area, and a small, but informative, museum. The Brick Store is located in the historic district of North Carolina's first state capital.

History
The Brick Store was built in 1840. It was named after the building material, which was mostly brick and mortar as opposed to wood and plaster.

After a series of financial hardships as the town became one of North Carolina's largest trading centers, the Brick Store fell into disrepair. By the middle of the twentieth century, most of the town's historic properties were threatened with demolition, and in 1959 the Brick Store was one of four properties, including the Old Warren County Courthouse, slated for destruction. A group of citizens banded together to save the Brick Store and other buildings including the Old Town Clerk's House and the Old Post Office. It was ultimately decided that the Brick Store was best suited for repurposing

Parker Millsap to perform at the Grand Theater on March 19
by: DARCIE LORENZO, KELSI JOBE and KELSEY HATCHER, WOWT
Riding a two-track trajectory through a music career that’s yielded five years of steady touring and an ever-growing fan base, the Oklahoma musician Parker Millsap is a singer-songwriter and guitarist whose latest record The Very Last Day is his second release on Big Picnic Records.
Born out of the aftermath of his critically-acclaimed 2016 LP The Very Last Day, this new work reflects on the small and sometimes insignificant moments in life that can provide a window into your inner self (and the world in which we live). The Very Last Day is the most vulnerable album to date, with every track telling a story from Parker’s life and what he’s been through in the last several years.
“The Very Last Day’s a story about love, regret, healing, and learning to love yourself again,” Parker says. “In the past I would’ve just dealt with these negative, painful things in life and not let myself love myself. I didn’t have the confidence in myself to love myself. In this

This past weekend I went to see the exhibit that is going on now in the new Tropical Forest Gallery. It is called "Symbioses", and it is the second collaboration between the Smithsonian and the National Geographic Society. It is a show that explores the relationships between nature and the people who live in it. It uses visualizations and graphics to help convey the ideas and concepts being explored. The visuals were especially interesting because some of them, such as the ones dealing with the relationships between certain flowers and bats or butterflies and moths, were based not simply on visuals but on what scientists actually know that such relationships truly exist. For instance, in one of the exhibits, a scientist actually showed how bats in the rain forest in Peru make their living. The point was that a lot of animals in the rain forest, including these bats, cannot see but rather rely on sounds to find their food. These sounds are a crucial part of all animal's lives as they help animals communicate and detect dangerous situations.
In a similar way, my visit to the Tropical Forest Gallery helped me to better analyze some of the ways that the visual world can help us to understand the concepts that are in textbooks, or which can help us to teach students

The latest data shows more than 60% of employers in the U.S. have implemented flexible work arrangements. A study reveals that a whopping 80% of employers want to maintain flexibility, yet only 27% actually do it well.
To help you build a framework to implement the flexibility initiative, I’d like to share my Top Three Takeaways from the International Foundation’s research, which surveyed more than 400 flexible work program leaders across the country.
We were able to break down the key findings of the survey into specific tips for business leaders who want to implement these initiatives.
If your organization allows flexible work arrangements, your workers will be in the driver’s seat, and it’s your job to make them successful. And when leaders help employees make work fit their lives, they become more productive at work and, more notably, more productive as a leader who creates and cultivates a team culture.
The biggest challenges faced in creating and maintaining flexible work is how it impacts productivity and retention.
The key to these problems is to have a strong strategic and operational plan in place that supports these programs. For example, the operational strategy is the plan for managing the day-to-day needs, while the strategic plan is the goal for the company

Rural-dwellers who live in an environmentally-sensitive area should not be deprived of their right to access the internet on the pretext that it may cause harm and disruption to biodiversity, says a new report released by the Human Rights Commission.
The Commission also said that Internet black spots were no excuse for the Government to fail to provide free access to the internet for every Māori, Pacific and low-income person.
The right to Internet access and free public internet access were guaranteed by the Human Rights Act, the report states.
The report, Who should pay for free public internet access?
includes case studies of communities in remote parts of New Zealand, including the Chatham Islands, who face connectivity challenges. It suggests that connectivity solutions should include free broadband that is available in the home or community.
"The Human Rights Act is about the freedom of choice that people have in how they spend their time. It includes time spent online. For example, free public access to the Internet is guaranteed by the Act, which prevents public authorities or telecommunications companies from discriminating against those who rely on access," said Commissioner Dr Inia Serupa.
The report points to a range of different models, such as the use of libraries or of the internet cafes

Facing an Uncertain Future
The State of the World’s Indigenous Peoples 2016
By Elizabeth Fraser
An estimated 370 million indigenous peoples worldwide live in over 90 countries spread across all continents, with the largest populations in Asia, America and Africa. However, many are spread across the world – sometimes in small groups – without being formally recognized as an indigenous peoples. They face similar, complex realities that affect their ability to thrive. Indigenous peoples are the only social group on the planet whose rights are being threatened daily by the very governments supposedly tasked with upholding them. This is particularly concerning at a time when governments are pushing for economic growth at all costs. If this continues, human rights violations against the indigenous population are likely to intensify in the coming years, as well as increasing their vulnerability to climate change effects, including extreme weather events, which could make indigenous lives harder and more unpredictable. This year, we look at their continued struggle.
On 5th March this year, the UN held its 8th session of the Permanent Forum on Indigenous Issues. Its theme this year was “Indigenous peoples, the UN, and climate change: from victims to actors”. The importance of a discussion of this nature is clear. Climate change, as is the case with many

Often considered to be one of the most beautiful gardens in England, Wanderings and Discoveries – The Garden of Sensory Stimulation has been designed and created by the author, illustrator and designer of over 130 books for children, John R. Green. He began this journey in 2001.
John spent five years searching for the site, then another 15 planning the garden for maximum sensory impact. A team of craftsmen including craftsmen with disabilities came together to bring this special space to life. John is passionate about accessibility for all abilities.
The garden is laid out with areas for different sensory experiences, including a water garden, a woodland path (with some sections underground), a wild bird maze, a garden path for wheelchair access, and a garden room. This is a garden that every child should be able to enjoy.
Wanderings and Discoveries – The Garden of Sensory Stimulation, is a unique sensory garden, a place to be enjoyed by young and old alike, which can be easily enjoyed by many people living with impairments.

A New Era For The US Military's Cyber Command
As its former name - Joint Force headquarters- reflects, U.S. Cyber Command (USCYBERCOM) has played an integral part in military and intelligence operations against America's foreign enemies for nearly 20 years. The Command's focus has always been on protecting the nation's most valuable computer networks, and ensuring that their users are not being harmed by cyber attacks.
USCYBERCOM has been involved in some of the most dramatic military cyber operations since 9/11, and its role has expanded even more in today's complex national security environment.
Today, there are more than 20,000 USCYBERCOM military members, a number that is more than five times larger than when it officially became the nation's first cyber military unit in 2009 (See: U.S. Cyber Command's Evolution to the Current World Order).
In order to keep pace with technology, the Command has also evolved its cyber military unit - including its ability to recruit, train, deploy and support cyber warriors.
For instance, the Command moved from focusing on the network layer to the transport layer in 2012, and it established its own cyber force to protect the U.S. government's cloud and mobility infrastructures.

The Bermuda Sun has a weekly page entitled “Wife Suggests Tie Me to Chair”. I am not the editor of this section of the newspaper, so if you read it and think of a subject for me to write about, just let me know – it is always in the Sunday edition.
There have been some wonderful suggestions which I would never have thought of.
The best suggestion for me was when a guy wrote in with a suggestion which was more along the lines of something he would NOT do. He was a “Mr. Type O” and in the article wrote something like “Why I Don’t Have a Wife”. If you read the article, you will see that his “O” blood type saved his life from many things – but his type O may be the reason that he did not marry.
In a column entitled “The Lighter Side of Life”, Mr. Richard C. Hinkle wrote “I don’t have a wife, because my blood type is O”.
“O” and all the other blood types are determined by the presence or absence of the ABO antigen. Most red blood cells carry the ABO antigen on their membrane surface. It is this antigen which distinguishes one blood type from the other

Honda has been making a big deal about all-new-for-2016 model changes to its Civic. This is an excellent move to keep the car competitive for years to come.
The changes are certainly not drastic in many aspects, but there are plenty of important and welcomed improvements. The most impactful one is a significant and overall size reduction for the Civic. That’s great.
The changes continue to be more evolutionary than revolutionary, with the best new parts including the engine, transmission and ride and handling. So they work together to make something that seems like a nice step up for the outgoing model yet not a drastic new leap. If you go with an automatic transmission, you’ll appreciate the changes quite a lot.

Birdwatching in the South West
For anyone who enjoys seeing birds, or bird-watching, the South West of England has plenty to offer.
It’s the perfect place to explore an area, and with its mild winters and warm summers, it’s a place that’s also loved by ramblers. Many of the parks, woodlands and wetlands in the South West make a fantastic place to spot the region’s rich variety of birdlife.
The South West has a wealth of woodlands, meadows and wetlands. The largest freshwater lake in England, Windermere, is in the area, as is the largest estuary in the country, the Tamar. It’s a bird-lover’s paradise.
The Pembrokeshire Coast National Park is a stunning place to spot all sorts of avians, with Skomer Island being a particular hotspot. The island is famous for the Puffins that nest there, along with many more species. Ariel: The Adventures of a Boy Who Couldn't Talk
Author: Liane Curtis
Ariel is a little boy who lives at a school for children unable to talk.
He is very happy there and always knows what to say.
This is a lovely book, a real must for toddlers.
The story is about a boy called Ben, who lives in a world where animals all speak, and humans just look.
After he moves to this new school he meets a girl called Holly.
Holly is the first one to talk to Ben but he doesn't quite know how to speak to her.
He doesn't know what to say to her, he just wanted to tell her how he felt for her to say, or what he can say.
This book is good and it will make you laugh as you read through the story, and at the end of the story you might even laugh too.
My favourite part is at the end when "the princess says she loves me very much".
If you have not read this book I suggest you do!

A new study suggests that people who eat a lot of fish may have a lower risk of developing dementia.
The new study, which was carried out at the University of Cambridge, analysed data from 6.3 million people aged between 50 and 71 in the UK Biobank cohort study, from 2006 to 2010.
The study compared how often participants ate fish with how much memory impairment they showed in a series of cognitive tests. They found that the amount fish the participants ate was one of the strongest predictors of higher performance on the tests, after an age of 65.
The researchers also looked at the relationship between dementia and the Omega-3 fat DHA, and found that while higher DHA blood levels were associated with a lower risk of dementia, other omega-3 fatty acids may not have a similar effect.
Dementia is a term used for a set of symptoms that may appear in middle and later stages of life, and is characterised by gradual deterioration in mental abilities, and problems with memory, thinking and communication.
It currently affects around 47 million people worldwide, and this figure is expected to almost triple by 2050 as the population ages.

Hospitalizations rise as Trump tests positive for virus
By LISA MASCARO, ALEXANDRA JAFFE, TAMARA LUSH and ZEKE MILLER
President Donald Trump was taken Wednesday to Walter Reed National Military Medical Center after becoming sick with the coronavirus, a decision the White House said might have been influenced by a lack of confidence in the ability of the White House to keep him safe.
The move to the military hospital drew immediate praise on both sides of the aisle, from those who suggested it was a sensible precaution even if the 78-year-old president wasn’t seriously ill and from conservatives who have been harshly critical of government efforts to contain the spread of the virus.
Trump left the White House on foot Wednesday evening, without commenting on reporters who yelled questions. He was carrying his mask, but it wasn’t clear if he was wearing it. Several White House staff were carrying out the same walk with their masks visible.
The White House on Friday had announced that Trump had tested positive, though officials did not mention any symptoms at the time.

The kitchen at the Aviary produces a range of small plates based on locally grown and sourced ingredients.
The Aviary is open Thursday to Monday from 5pm to midnight.

Local food is all the rage and there are those who are going for gold.
With the launch of this blog site, I intend to be part of something much more modest: celebrating the people in food who are taking positive steps towards the regeneration of our local economies through a commitment to local food and the revitalisation of local communities.
I’ve been involved with food in one way or another for just over 25 years.

I started working on a voluntary basis for The Community Shop (which later became the Foodhall Project) as a volunteer. I was soon recruited and worked through the ranks ending in a short-lived managing directorship before I became a full-time freelance consultant.

Sacrificial Trench Drain
Sacrificial trench drainage systems are specifically designed to divert drainage water from a sloping or high-risk area by gravity into a pre-buried drain where the water can be controlled. The water can be removed by pumping to another surface or in a sewer system, if one is available.
The advantages of the system are the high reliability of the soil slope and a lower amount of surface water. This makes a smaller ditch with fewer and more manageable maintenance requirements.
The system can be used when the area of the slope has a small or moderate gradient and the water level is low or when a large area of the sloping ground is high, sloping land. The system is mainly applied to farmland in the country, to protect the land from soil erosion and flooding, when the existing natural slope would pose a safety hazard.
Sacrificial trench drainage systems for agricultural use with a natural slope of less than 6% are not covered by this EU Directive. If the ground is higher than 3 m then a system with reinforced walls must be used, which is in line with other EU rules (WFD).

Diane de Poitiers (25 October 1514–17 October 1572) was the Duchess of Valentinois. She was a favorite companion and lifelong friend of Catherine de Medici.
A descendant of both the powerful Clisson and Auvergne families, Diane de Poitiers was born in Nogent-le-Rotrou, one of the few Norman towns to escape being captured by the invading English during the Hundred Years' War.[1] Her father was Antoine de Vergy, Sire de Poitiers, who was of modest nobility but who had managed to retain his rank during the wars. He supported Francis I in his rise to power and received an appointment at the court of the new king. The future duchess made her appearance on 25 October 1514.[1]
Diane's mother, Jeanne de Valois, daughter of Louis XII and his second wife Anne de Bretagne[2], was the eldest surviving child of the Valois dynasty from the branch of the House of Valois-Bourbon which would produce Francois I. Moravian (sometimes called Herrnhuter) was the first formal denomination to settle in the Holy Land, founding Bethlehem in 1720. The name Moravia refers not to an ethnic group but to the area around Brno, in present-day Czechia. The group arrived at the invitation of a local Arab notary, al-Amarna, who believed that the arrival of Moravians would aid the Christian revival of the Holy Land. In 1736, a group of Moravian missionaries established the first missionary station in Bethlehem, followed by additional missions and villages in other regions of Palestine.
With its roots in the German Lutheran tradition, the movement was part of the rise of Pietism in the early 18th century. I am thrilled to have a spot on the Kensington Tours Blog Tour for A Painted House by Laura Ingalls Wilder, courtesy of William Morrow.
As a lifelong reader and fan of Laura Ingalls Wilder, I was excited to have the chance to review this book.
One thing that immediately struck me about this book is the lack of pictures and illustrations. For those not familiar with Laura Ingalls Wilder and her work, I would highly suggest picking up some of her books if you still haven’t. The Ingalls family was very lucky to have been photographed in countless black and white films, which were preserved for posterity, and these pictures can be found in their original forms as well as reprints throughout bookstores across America. While these stills may look a bit old and archaic to some today, they are still a visual history of the past as seen through the eyes of an amazing author.
In a sense, Laura has been frozen in time, an eternal child that continues to captivate us and our children, who are fascinated every time they flip the pages of one of her books, wondering what the next thing Laura Wilder tells us will be.

The International Society of Aesthetic Plastic Surgery (ISAPS) and its partner company ISAPS World have agreed to pay up on unfulfilled promises.
ISAPS has agreed to pay $4 million over claims of using membership and certification fees to run personal accounts and investments.
International Society of Aesthetic Plastic Surgery (ISAPS) chairman, Dr Thomas Haeberle, resigned in April 2015 to “safeguard the Society’s assets at the time of its dissolution as I was its director and president, and was aware of the use of these assets”.
Haeberle is reported to have said that the ISAPS was forced to file for bankruptcy, after being unable to pay investors for losses and creditors.
ISAPS World has also been charged, as it “did not keep membership and certification fees separate from funds invested by members".
Both Haeberle and ISAPS World's directors and board members have been fired.
The Society has been operating as a non-profit organization since 2000, for the purpose of certifying medical professionals in aesthetic and reconstructive plastic surgery and improving patient standards internationally.

The Horticultural Collection (HC) was established in 1980, with the acquisition of a small collection of orchids, which was subsequently expanded (see Orchid Inventory). From 1982 until 2012 the collection was maintained and cultivated in the Greenhouse at the National Botanic Gardens. In May 2012 the collection was relocated within the Irish Whiskey and Food Experience at the Jameson Distillery, New Midleton, Co. Cork, under guidance of the then Curator, Dr. John Beaman. In late 2014, with the opening of the Jameson Experience – Midleton on the Distillery Site, the management of the HC was transferred to this museum.
The HC is a living collection, with an emphasis on the culture and management of a collection of plant genera and species that are endemic to, or well adapted to, the Midleton area. These are the genus Diphyscium, the genus Eryngium and the genus Anigozanthos. In addition to these genera, a small number of plants were incorporated to reflect other plant habitats across Ireland.

What a Mess
by Paul Ortiz
A few weeks back, as he was preparing to deliver the annual State of the Union speech, President Obama proposed an immigration reform bill. The White House suggested that immigrants should receive a path to citizenship if they meet certain requirements and if they learn English and become “fully engaged in the life of the nation”; the president even cited the importance of providing immigrant children the right to attend public school free of charge.
The president was right on many counts: immigrants, especially those who’ve been here for decades, need a better deal; a path to citizenship for undocumented immigrants is crucial; and it is simply wrong to send immigrant children to school only after they’ve paid millions in cash fees.
But the Obama call for reform wasn’t nearly enough. And it wasn’t meant to be. The president’s initiative was simply a “framework” bill, a preliminary step, not to be taken seriously—that is until it was taken seriously by the very lawmakers who were in the room. “We don’t have a bipartisan solution yet, but the discussion was positive and constructive,” said Senator Jeff Flake, a Republican of Arizona.

Tobias, David. “Terrorism in the Twenty-First Century: Beyond Bin Laden”. In: Journal of Conflict Resolution 53, no. 9 (2009), pp. 1377–1308
Tobias (2009) is among the seminal works of the ‘new terrorism studies’ literature. The book is a response to the 9/11 terrorist attack and takes stock of the main trends in the field of terrorism studies since this event, including the debate on counter-terrorist policy, the study of ‘new’ types of terrorism, especially the study of state terror and its role and definition, and terrorism in relation to social movements such as terrorism in Latin America.
The book provides a very comprehensive analysis of the literature on terrorism and counter-terrorist policy, covering almost the entire field of terrorism studies. It includes some original essays on terrorism. Tobias’ main thesis is that terrorism research is not only shaped by the field’s dominant paradigms, but also that this process is shaped by the field’s relationship to policy – the relationship between experts and the public, policy and research is key. Tobias calls for more interaction between academics and policy makers but warns against any idea that the policy-literature is untrustworthy,

What is the Best Time to Start My Fertility Treatment?
When you talk to your doctor about starting a fertility treatment, it generally takes time for them to analyze your test results and decide when is the best time.
This is the stage where a lot of work gets done, not just on your treatment, but your mental preparation as well. For the most part, a lot of people say the earlier they jump on treatment the better.
There’s no better time to take action than now!
What Happens When You Start?
You’ll be asked to come in regularly for blood tests, ultrasounds, and hormone tests, and you’ll have to get your blood drawn a few times a week. The hormones you receive will be at high-risk for clumping, so you’ll have to be very careful in what you do when you get home with them.
A lot of these tests go through you getting a positive test for pregnancy. This is done to rule out the possibility of a false positive. This usually comes after six weeks of treatments but can come even earlier.
If something goes wrong, there will be a backup system for getting sperm in case they need to do an emergency transfer.

Egypt has released nine journalists, some of whom have said they were tortured and abused while in pretrial detention, in the biggest mass release by President Abdel-Fattah el-Sissi’s government since its 2015 crackdown.
The journalists, including four who were detained for more than four months on charges related to the reporting of violence in the southern town of Nagaa Hammadi, arrived at Cairo's Heliopolis district on Sunday after being cleared for release.
Several journalists also said they had been tortured in detention, according to a lawyer for four of the former detainees and a security official involved in the case. Those officials did not want to be publicly identified.
Four of the former journalists were sentenced to prison terms ranging from two years and three months to seven years.
"They called out to each other after they got off the van.... They were all crying and hugging each other and weeping," the lawyer said.
Another journalist who was released had been on a hunger strike. The security official said the journalist was brought to hospital after being released.
It was not clear why some of the journalists were released.

Nursing students help the sick
WARSAW, Ind. —
Nursing students at the Indiana University-Purdue University at Fort Wayne School of Nursing have become an active force in the community in the last two years.
After Hurricane Katrina affected Louisiana and Mississippi in late August 2005, the Fort Wayne residents were moved to action.
With the support of local and international humanitarian agencies, nursing students and faculty raised funds that were donated in August to the victims of the disaster. The funds included donations received from nursing programs at IPFW, the Fort Wayne and Marion alumni associations, student organizations and individual donors.
This year, after learning of the plight of families affected by the tsunamis and the earthquakes in the southern and southeast Asian regions, students organized a community outreach drive.
“Our first collection was held in the beginning of March, and it continues this April,” said Amy Lusher, assistant professor and nursing program director. “The purpose of the drive is for them to become educated about the needs of those affected by these disasters, so they can understand the importance of providing assistance to our international brothers and sisters.”

Why is it important to maintain lawns?
There are several factors that influence whether a lawn will grow green.
Sun: If your lawn is exposed to the sun, it may not be the healthiest option if you wish to have a thick, green lawn all summer.
Soil: If your soil isn't a good mix of sand, peat and clay, a large area may not be possible. Lawn care requires special products that will work well with your soil.
Shade: Certain parts of the garden are exposed to direct sunlight. Maintaining a lawn in a shady area requires planning and preparation so that the soil and lawn is able to withstand high temperatures.
Water: Most types of garden grass need at least an inch of water each week to grow. If the water is over three inches deep at each time of the season, the lawn will be fine.

The faculty is comprised of both active practitioners and researchers in multiple fields who bring their different disciplinary perspectives to marine science research issues. Each year, the Department of Earth, Skua and Marine Science supports approximately 300 graduate students and 300 undergraduate students participating in cutting-edge research throughout the state, as well as an active research center and museum that serves as an educational resource for Florida citizens.
The University of Florida also houses the Florida Climate Institute Center for Oceanic and Atmospheric Research. Founded in 1984, the Florida Climate Institute (FCI) provides the research infrastructure and funding for interdisciplinary research on climate change. The Center for Oceanic and Atmospheric Research (COAR) was established in 1987 under the aegis of FCI.

Book Collectors:
Rare Book School is a major publisher of rare books and historical texts for scholars and collectors. For more than thirty years, the school has been offering professional and nonprofessional researchers, students and collectors the opportunity to study one-on-one with leading experts in the field. Since its inception in 1983, the school has been the preeminent scholarly meeting place and an international treasure trove and clearing house for rare and important books, manuscripts, and historic papers. Rare Book School courses of study include collecting, preservation and conservation, manuscript studies, bibliography, bookbinding and restoration, book design, writing and editing, and much, much more.
More than 3,100 alumni of the school have taken advantage of its courses. Dating from the 14th century, the town of San Severino Marche in the Ancona province of Marche, is home to an ornate and beautifully painted marble fountain. The fontane d'acqua is situated on the right of Via IV Novembre just before you enter the centre of the old town and near the ancient parish church of San Nicola.
The fontane d'acqua is located within a courtyard beside a lovely old tavern which can be visited by night.
The fontane d'acqua is in the courtyard of the old castle of San Severino Marche, which is now a luxury hotel. The hotel is named La Stazione, but it is probably best just to ignore this name.
The fontane d'acqua is accessed from the outside by a gate, but this only gives access to one of two courtyards.
I was not familiar with the history of the fontane d'acqua until I was shown some photos by a local. It seems that a fountain similar to this is recorded here in the old castle courtyard already in 1270AD. I cannot verify the historical accuracy of this, but this type of fountain was popular all over Italy in the 16th century.

The Awards are part of AHRC’s Film Competition programme.
Each year the Awards reward outstanding research into film and the moving image in all their formats, including documentary, feature film, animation and experimental film. These awards build on the success of the AHRC’s Research Film Awards, introduced in 2007–08.
Research Film of the Year 2020
Film: A Woman's Guide to Migration by Laura Dicken & Nkiru Nnawuchi
A woman’s guide to migration is a short film following the journey of a young Nigerian mother and her children who are living in Calais, in the hope of travelling to England. A Woman’s Guide to Migration was developed from a participatory filmmaking process using a documentary filmmaking framework. Film is a powerful way to engage and empower communities, and this documentary-led process enables people to share their perspective and experiences with others.

A simple explanation of the differences between IPv4 and IPv6 addresses. How the internet works, and why you need to be aware of the IPv6 transition.
Some say that the world came to a grinding halt when the world wide web was born in 1969 and this was true for a while. We had the Internet and some of its uses in the 80s, but in the years before the web was born the Internet was a strange, isolated and experimental network. Now the Internet is part of daily life for all of us. The internet as we know it, as a vast, interconnected and public network is based on the IP addressing system. The following is an overview of the IPv4 vs IPv6 addresses and what IPv6 is and how it will impact your internet access in the future.
What is IPv4 and how did it evolve?
IPv4 is the original internet protocol that was developed in the early 80s and it evolved from the ARPANET, and as the world went internet crazy in the 90s, IPv4 addresses were assigned and expanded. A new report by UK-based renewable energy consultancy Px Group and solar PV manufacturing firm Ampcontrol reveals that the global off-grid energy sector could be worth US$6.5 billion by 2027, representing a 10-fold increase from today’s figure.
Px Group estimates that the global distributed solar market has total energy sales of US$2.3 billion in 2018 and will be worth US$2.86 billion in 2027, reflecting a compound annual growth rate of 15 percent. But that doesn’t take into account the off-grid solar opportunity, with Px Group estimating that the sector could hit US$6.51 billion by 2027 with a CAGR of 14.6 percent.
These figures are based on the analysis of Ampcontrol’s off-grid module supplier database that provides information on module sales for projects in 18 counties including Afghanistan, Nepal, Bangladesh and India.
The consultancy’s study found that the off-grid energy sector in developing nations is set to experience strong growth, driven by increasing affordability of renewable energy and technological advancements.
In addition, the report identified a shift in the supply-chain, in which more modules are being supplied to off-grid and developing countries

In the mid ‘80s, the National Research Council of Canada (NRC) created a team of scientists from eight different disciplines to work on a project called the “Mosquito Project” – to create an effective mosquito-control agent. Their focus was the disease-bearing mosquito that thrives in humid conditions.
Mosquito Project
Over the next several years (1985 to 1987), the Mosquito Project produced several compounds in a “wet lab” inside the NRC in Ottawa, Canada. These compounds were tested on different mosquito species, and were highly toxic to mosquitoes, but were also highly toxic to humans.
They found, however, that the compounds caused the mosquitoes’ wings to break shortly after mating, rendering the eggs unusable. They then created an altered version of the compounds that were more stable and could be safely used to control mosquitoes.
Dr. John M. Kane of the University of Illinois and Dr. Thomas L. Moser of the United States Department of Agriculture tested the compounds on a species of mosquito known to cause West Nile virus (an often fatal illness that kills an estimated 20,000 people every year). The results were positive, leading the United States Department of Agriculture to provide funding to develop a mosquito-killing agent

A year in, my friends.
A new year, but with old friends — not the least of which is a new baby.
The holiday season is the best time to get together with old friends, new friends, family friends.
This past weekend, we had a reunion at my house for friends who were in town for the holidays.
I invited anyone who was in town for Christmas — even old girlfriends! — and was completely surprised at the response. I had at least half a dozen people show up, and the whole group spent most of the day on the couch with the new baby.

Merry Christmas, everyone! Hope you have a cozy holiday.

Rideau Valley Conservation Authority
The Rideau River is important to the people of Canada and to the people of the Rideau Valley region. The natural ecosystem provides many ecological services and recreational opportunities; it is an economic asset, it provides protection from natural hazards, and it serves as a source of inspiration, cultural meaning, health, and well-being.
In the Rideau river watershed, the RVC monitors aquatic health, consults with landowners and municipalities, manages and educates about the protection of natural heritage. As part of a network of conservation authorities and watershed associations, they are responsible for flood management and water-level control and protection. Together, watershed associations, conservation authorities and government organizations such as Environment Canada are working to balance the protection of these environmental services with other societal needs.
Conservation Authorities began in Ontario over 50 years ago. While initially focused on the management of natural resources, today’s Conservation Authorities also manage flood control, watershed health, water quality, and natural heritage.
In 2011, the Conservation Authorities Act received Royal Assent and it became the Ontario Conservation Authorities Act. The Act provides legal protection for the conservation authorities and their services (including flood management) and enables conservation authorities to continue to contribute to an

Sri Lanka: Authorities must not delay release of Sivaram
Sri Lanka, UPR Report - 2017,	UN High Commissioner for Human Rights
“Authorities must not delay release of human rights defender Sivaram”
The ICJ, Amnesty International and UN Human Rights Council (HRC) experts today called on Sri Lanka’s authorities to immediately and unconditionally release Sivaram Dargis, a human rights defender known to have recently suffered from torture while in detention in 2015.
The ICJ and its partners also expressed concern about the case of another detainee, Jalu Dassanayake, reported to have also suffered from torture whilst in secret detention in 2005. The HRC experts are due to visit Sri Lanka in November 2017 to assess the progress in addressing human rights concerns as identified in the report to the HRC for its Universal Periodic Review (UPR).
“We continue to receive reports about numerous allegations of torture and other cruel and degrading treatment of detainees in Sri Lanka, including allegations of enforced disappearances. Authorities must address these allegations by releasing those unlawfully detained and establishing effective legal, judicial and administrative remedies for victims of torture,” said Hugh Williamson, Director of the ICJ’

Jinju Jokbal is a traditional Korean rice cake. The rice cake itself is a mix of black sweet rice and chestnut flour. This Jokbal has a sweet, but not too sweet taste. You can eat it as is.
Jinju Jokbal is the type of rice bread or cake you eat throughout the year. Especially during the Lunar New Year when we cleanse our body by eating a rice cake after a meal.
Now Jokbal is very easy to make. Especially for those who don’t have a Kitchen Aid or have a small Kitchen Aid.
1. Cook your rice and set it aside.
2. Put the rice to the Kitchen Aid for 5-7 minutes so the husk of the rice is cooked.
3. Add the chestnut flour to a bowl. Use your hands to mix it. If you want a fine mixture add 1 tablespoon of rice powder or water.
4. Add the rice to the bowl and mix it well. Add the salt and the red bean paste. If you want, you can use green beans instead of red beans. The red bean paste must be warm. I put the rice mix in the fridge for 15 minutes. After that, mix it again

Martha M. Knutzen has been named Director of Institutional Advancement for Community Blood Services (CBS) in St. Paul, MN.
In this new position, which she began in July, Martha will oversee the planning, fundraising, marketing and advocacy for CBS, the leading provider of blood products to area physicians.
Martha’s appointment follows an interim period led by Executive Director John Jett, who stepped in after former Director Janice Gersch left CBS for personal reasons.
Before joining Community Blood Services in September of 2009, Martha oversaw the fundraising and marketing activities for the Hennepin County Medical Center Foundation. She played an active role in the HCMC’s fundraising efforts for many new and renovated facilities, including its Cancer Care Center.
Previously, she also served as Director of Marketing and Resource Development for the Minnesota Institute for Sickle Cell Disease, a nonprofit organization whose mission is to improve the quality of life for persons with sickle cell disease in Minnesota. Prior to that, Martha worked for 10 years for the Hennepin County Medical Center Foundation as the Director of Community Engagement, leading foundation projects and developing and implementing fund development tactics and strategies. She also managed foundation events.

Tallink Grupp has launched its new cruise service in Finland, offering the unique experience of travelling between Helsinki and the port of Tallinn in Estonia. The new cruise service is available to passengers of Tallink’s Silja Line, which sails between Helsinki and Tallinn, as well as to other cruise operators’ guests.
The route is approximately 90 km (56 mi) and one of its most beautiful parts is the Kupari canal, which is one of the largest artificial waterways in Europe. The cruise will be sailed on the unique M/V Nils Holgersson. M/V Nils Holgersson, designed by Swedish world famous architect Pål Gulliksen, sails between the mainland and Finland’s largest island, where it berths in Kupari. Passengers of M/V Nils holgersson will have the option to disembark at sea or enjoy the unique scenery by taking a walk through the Kupari canal, which is up to seven metres deep.

Nye County is home to large herds of elk, mule deer, and antelope and small herds of sheep and mohaves. Nevada has one of the largest elk herds in the United States, with thousands of elk in the state and up to 5,000 elk at large in the Fallon Wildlife Management Area. In 2017 the Nevada Department of Wildlife moved over 2,000 elk from the Walker Creek Elk Area to the Fallon Wildlife Management Area.
Nevada is also home to over 30 mule deer herds and several antelope herds throughout the state.
Nye County is unique for its habitat for black-tailed deer and mule deer, mohaves, fennec fox, wild horses, and elk. In recent years more than 25,000 fennec fox have been captured. Fennec foxes are native to northern Africa, the Middle East and the Arabian

In the News Today: A Stalker or a Friend?
By Jessica Bennett @jesscbennettJuly 14, 2011
Today, the AP reported allegations of an affair between an anonymous Republican fund raising executive in Wisconsin and a former colleague. The woman, R. J. Johnson, alleges that the Republican executive began making inappropriate contact with her soon after she ended an affair with the CEO of an adjacent building.
But who were these alleged lovers? The AP’s reporting was based largely on information from R. J.’s Facebook page and her blog. But these sources were later linked to a Republican fundraiser who was a prominent member of the state Republican Party and helped Governor Scott Walker raise funds during this year’s recall elections. Her name is Sarah Wiersma — and she’s married to the former Milwaukee mayor after whom another fund raising business was named. Sarah Wiersma’s ties to the governor are obvious, and she is one of the governor’s biggest fund raisers.
The AP should have waited to publish until the story was 100% verified, and it should have made sure to get Wiersma’s side of the story. Not running the story was the responsible decision, given Wiersma’s position

The Sustainable Forestry Initiative Inc. is a voluntary organisation established to promote the economic, environmental and social well-being of society in a manner consistent with ecological balance and individual responsibility.
The Sustainable Forestry Initiative® (SFI®) programs are designed to assist Canadian forest sector businesses achieve their environmental, social and economic objectives. Canadian forest sector businesses, which include forestry operations, and the manufacturers and traders in their networks have come together in partnership to create solutions-based environmental certification programs that benefit communities and the companies who work to make the system a success.
SFI’s innovative and science-based solutions encourage improved forest management practices and help companies measure and report on progress, enabling them to make continuous improvements toward a green industry. And through its many local initiatives and community partnerships, its programs empower people through education, training and employment.
The SFI Forest Management Standard is based on science and performance, and reflects best available forest management practices as well as performance expectations to ensure sustainable forest management. As Canada’s national forest certification system recognized as an independently valid third-party certification standard (the first forest certification standard in North America to obtain such recognition), SFI standards drive continuous improvement and are the most widely adopted by foresters, naturalists,

The best way to learn about nature is to observe a creature in action, in the wild, says Kevin Dyer, an extension professor of animal science at UMass who teaches courses on invasive species. “What is the animal’s behavior?” he says. “Does it eat certain foods or hunt certain things? What do its behaviors signify?”
When I was growing up, there was little opportunity for the kind of outdoor exploration into nature that Dyer describes; I mostly stayed inside reading about frogs, salamanders and moths, and I would often dream of going beyond the backyard and into the wild. But as I got more active in conservation, I started to take a real interest in what it’s like to interact with the wild world.

A recent article in the Telegraph highlights the issue of the growing use of technology in our lives and how they are influencing us. The article suggests that technology is an issue in education and for our mental health. While there is evidence to support this, there is less evidence to support the suggestion of a causal effect between technology and negative aspects of our lives.
It’s a bit of a stretch to link technological development with a loss in face-to-face communication and a decline in our emotional intelligence. The two are more likely to have been intertwined for thousands of years, and the way we use technology today is no different.
The truth is, technology isn’t going anywhere, and that includes our phones, tablets and all the other digital tools and platforms we use on a daily basis. In fact, we’d be in a world of trouble without technology. In the workplace, we rely on technology so heavily that many feel they don’t know how to function without it.
So should we stop using technology? I’m not about to go off on about how smartphones and technology are ruining our lives as some people have stated, but I do believe every now and again it’s important to take a step back, reassess whether technology

It would take three or four lifetimes to experience all the exciting things to do in Sydney. And that’s just on land. There’s also the world-class dining, stunning beaches, and lively nightlife. But first, there are the iconic landmarks that dot the city, like the Opera House and the Harbor Bridge, which are must-sees.
The history of the city is rich with stories. Explore the old gaol on Millers Point, which was built in 1796. Walk through the Botanic Gardens of Millers Point to see old colonial-era houses and a lighthouse built in 1816.
Cruise along the Harbour and take in the view of the Sydney Opera House in all its glory. Stroll through the cobblestone laneways of The Rocks, where you’ll hear stories of convicts and European settlers. And take the famous Bondi to Coogee walk along the coastline. It’s a beautiful 6.8-kilometer (4-mile) walk, so even a day will probably not be enough to experience it all.

Kazuo Matsunaga, the former mayor of Kyoto (1988-2003) and former ambassador to the United Nations, has been named chair of a new committee aimed at bringing Osaka into the spotlight as the next major Japanese city. The committee, “Osaka City is Japan,” was created by Osaka Mayor Toru Hashimoto to boost the city’s international profile.
The committee includes members of the Japan Association of Corporate Executives, and the Japan Business Federation among others.
While Osaka is often overlooked by travelers, it is actually the country’s second-largest city after Tokyo, and it still maintains much of the old world charm and culture that drew Japanese business people to the pre-war capital.
Japan is a key destination for luxury retail and luxury accommodation brands but there is a need for cities to create and support unique local experiences that visitors remember. We see the creation of this body to act as a catalyst for development in Osaka as a positive sign, as this is the best way to put Osaka firmly on the map and drive further demand from both domestic and international visitors.

In a world filled with superheroes, Starman was one of the greatest of all time, but when he tried to return from the dead to rescue his wife he got more than he bargained for. Now a vagabond in space, this last of the World Knights will have to find a way to regain his lost powers and save his beloved. But in the midst of the apocalypse that would lead to his death did he learn one last lesson… that even a superhero’s life is worth the loss of death; that sometimes a hero must save the world even if he won’t have to live with the consequences.
Starman is a comic written by Dan Jurgens and illustrated by Jerry Ordway. It is the follow up series to the Death of Superman storyline that occurred in the late 1990s. It was published by DC Comics and ran for 25 issues. It takes place after Superman's death and focuses on the legacy he left behind. It was published from October 1999 to July 2002.

The Newest Wrinkle in the Great Wall of China
By Andrew Chow, | Fox News
The Great Wall of China. (Image courtesy of GreatWallOfChinaHistory.org)
You might think the world's most famous wall has a lot to protect. But now, a new threat is haunting the Great Wall of China: boredom.
"When I came to Beijing, in 1992, it was still so quiet," said artist Zhang Cheng. "Even the Great Wall was quiet. It was like a lonely desert."
But the city's ever-growing population has changed that — and, for those who want their own piece of China's history, it has created a new niche industry. There are now Great Wall hotels, restaurants and theme parks, and Beijing has even produced its own Great Wall film, starring superstars Jackie Chan and Gong Li.
"Now, many Chinese people come to the Great Wall for leisure. They come to relax. Or maybe because they want to do some souvenir, or to do some special holiday," Zhang said.

Bellevue is a city in and the county seat of Platte County, Kansas, United States. As of the 2010 census, the city population was 1,972. The city includes the small community of Belle Plaine which was first incorporated in 1875 and had a population of 1,733 in 2010.The city was named after Belle Plaine, Missouri, by Thomas Kemp, a businessman who also founded the community of Belle Plaine, Kansas. Belle Plaine, Kansas was named for Kemp's wife.
Sawdust Hill is a neighborhood in northeast Bellevue. The neighborhood is north of the Crossroads, south of Downtown Kansas City, and east of I-35.

The first house and store was built by John Roebling in the first half of the 19th-century. Soon after, sawmill operations began in the area. Sawdust Hill became a suburb of Kansas City in the late-1900s. The neighborhood is known for its large homes, the many trails for walking, and its proximity to Downtown.

Location
Sawdust Hill is located in northeast Bellevue. The neighborhood is bordered by Interstate 35, a creek running alongside Tiffany St., the Crossroads District in Kansas City

Kilkenny Castle
KILKENNY, COUNTY KILKENNY
(Old Castle - O'Dempsey)
The castle we see now was built in the 18th century but an earlier castle (O'Dempsey) stood nearby on the site of a 9th century motte. It belonged to the O'Dempsey family, the last of whom died in 1586, when it came into the possession of the Butler family of the castle. In 1666 it was given to Richard Newdigates, and in 1688 it was taken over by the Duke of Ormonde. Its importance was increased considerably in 1691, when all the troops in the province were gathered in Kilkenny for active service against the rebellious Jacobite army that had been left in Ireland after the Battle of the Boyne. James II came here to surrender and after the Treaty of Limerick, 1691 (the treaty by which he surrendered the crown to William of Orange), Kilkenny was made the headquarters of the English administration. Fiona McLeod on why Scotland should follow the example of Portugal, Spain and France and invest in public transport
As the economy slumps, politicians, business leaders and civic society all agree that it’s infrastructure that will help kick-start the recovery. In the case of the UK, it is infrastructure investment that has been woefully inadequate. A lack of road, rail and other connectivity has long been the bugbear of Scotland, and a major barrier to economic growth.
Scotland is in a good position to rectify this, given the massive savings it has made in road network costs following the introduction of Congestion Charging in Edinburgh. The money saved could be better put to use to boost infrastructure in Scotland.
In fact, a recent study published by the think-tank the National Infrastructure Commission (as part of the Future Cities Commission) concluded that an extra £500 billion a year in infrastructure investment could unlock the UK economy to the tune of £2 trillion in increased productivity.

Tesoro has not announced a date to resume operations.

Tamara Renee is a Los Angeles-based, self-taught artist who was born on a military base but spent a good portion of her life living abroad in Germany, Japan, and Belgium. Although an American, art was instilled in her life from a young age through education and community. Growing up, being artistic was always a part of her life and she remembers creating art with her mother and grandfather; they encouraged not to sell, but to make the work their own.
Tamara Renee’s work embodies color, light, space and texture, and has a focus on the female form with an emphasis on self-love. Tamara Renee is a master with mediums such as collage, painting, and mixed media. She uses found objects in her work and uses her own image to create visual narratives. She captures emotion through symbolism, texture, color, composition, and placement within an abstract and modern art style. Her work consists of a variety of subject matter and media that ranges from traditional to conceptual.

The K2-XPO-2 is a new single band filter for broadband, high-power applications including satellite transmitters operating from 0.6 cm to 23.1 cm wavelengths in the Ku/C-band and the L-band and broadband high-power amplifiers in the S-, C- and L-band for mobile phones and base stations.
The filter design is optimised to provide best value product with higher transmittance in the Ku-band compared to other high power single band Ku band filters currently available in the market. The K2-XPO-2 filter is capable of handling the new stringent design requirements for C-band satellite transmissions. The filter is also able to meet the stringent requirements for the L-band mobile phone applications. The K2-XPO-2 is designed to operate with up to 200 dB OFA. It is characterised by high transmission from 50 cm to 13,500 km and at high power. The filter has low attenuation for the satellite and mobile phone applications.
The K2-XPO-2 is optimised for high efficiency applications, with lower insertion loss compared to competitive designs with 50% higher power handling compared to conventional designs, including a significant difference over the 1 dB-stopband.

Transport and urban regeneration in the UK and the Baltic countries
An economic and environmental assessment
Author: James McKinnon
Since the beginning of the 21st century, the Baltic states have undertaken a successful process of rapid development and a radical transformation of their transport landscape from predominantly rail to road transport, with the aim of creating more sustainable cities with vibrant economies. It seems like there's an app for just about everything we do now--we can pay bills, pay for parking, get directions, even locate nearby gyms or food trucks.
It might seem like we've reached peak convenience with all the available options--but if any of these services get hacked, we run the risk of losing thousands of dollars to con artists or data thieves.
While these services are often protected by firewalls, they are only so secure, and the consequences of losing the data or information in such an app can affect your reputation.
But what if hackers took all of your important personal information without you knowing about it? They would likely use the information to steal your identity.
Identity theft is the fastest rising crime in the country. Here are the things to do if you suspect your information has been stolen by a hacker.
Change all of your passwords. Even if the information wasn't directly stolen, hackers were likely able to get a look at your password information. They can now crack those simple passwords and potentially do even more damage. Don't let it happen--just go through account passwords and make sure there are no obvious flaws--weak, common or easy to guess passwords. Use different, strong passwords for different sites and accounts.

‘We Are The World’: A song and video for a very specific moment |
With so many live dates and festivals cancelled this year, the classical music industry has had to rely on digital premieres and concert performances recorded at home. And while they may not have been broadcast on PBS or BBC Radio 3, they have been vital to musicians and audiences alike: one of the more uplifting, but also more poignant, was We Are The World — the official 1985 video, in which singers and musicians came together virtually to produce an uplifting and urgent anthem, just over four weeks before Live Aid.
The video was the brainchild of producer Quincy Jones, who had originally intended to stage a fundraising concert for Africa, but was forced to cancel due to the AIDS epidemic spreading throughout the country. The original ‘We Are the World’ lineup included musicians from all over the world, including Ray Charles, Bruce Springsteen, Diana Ross, Stevie Wonder and Paul Simon. The track was originally a cover of the charity single from USA for Africa, which became the biggest-selling single in music history.

Brief Overview of C++
Filed Under: Programming C++
C and C++
C is an old, general-purpose language that's been used to program a wide variety of devices since back in 1964. It is now the defacto standard that is used in many embedded systems. However, it isn't used to program microcomputers as C++ is. C was developed initially by a research group at Berkeley called The Center For Language and Information Studies.
It was designed to be a general-purpose language which is as expressive as any other. This was intended to allow programmers to quickly develop programs for a wide variety of platforms with minimal changes. It was a little ahead of its time, and didn't quite catch on as fast as other languages like BASIC and Pascal.
This general-purpose language had some serious problems. A basic program in C consists of the source code that is compiled by the C compiler into an object file. The object file can then be linked with any other object file that is required by the program. Programs written in this way can be copied and run on a wide variety of architectures that have nothing to do with each other.
The problem is that the same program can be copied and run two different operating

All cargo shipments via China-Europe freight train services on Thai railways will continue to function during the holidays with freight trains operating to and from Europe via the Laohuachi Railway hub.
There will be four passenger trains between Thailand and China each day, including four to China's east coast via Vietnam and two to Kunming via Laos.

The past week in Washington, DC, has been the first since the midterm elections with a government open once again, although the shutdown has not yet been officially ended. There are more government employees than ever before to be furloughed, however. The shutdown’s full impact won’t become apparent until the government goes on furlough. What effect does the shutdown have on oil prices and the market for crude oil?
Crude oil prices have stabilized after the short-lived drop that occurred when news of a deal that would reopen the government reached markets in the middle of this week. That deal could have had the potential to result in the loss of hundreds of billions of dollars and the disruption of oil production in North America if the House of Representatives and the Senate had failed to agree on a deal. The deal that was brokered by John Boehner and Harry Reid didn’t result in an end to the shutdown, nor is it expected that it will lead to the resolution of the debt ceiling debate. However, it did buy time. The Eternal City: Rome
Rome, the capital of Italy and former home of the Roman Empire and the Renaissance is one of the top tourist destinations in the world. Rome’s history dates to the 8th century BC when the population of the city, according to legend, was established by the twin brothers Romulus and Remus. One of the great wonders of the world is the Colosseum, which had seated 50,000 people and was the site of the gruesome games. Built in 80-82 AD, it was the largest amphitheater ever built. Today the site is still called the Colosseum. At one time, two-thirds of the population of Rome lived in brick and mortar buildings, the rest living in tents. The Roman Colosseum The city became the center of the Catholic Church under Pope Leo III (papacy 847-855). The Popes lived and exercised much power within the great palace called the Palazzo Vaticano. Aquifoliaceae is a family of about 200 genera and more than 2000 species of flowering plants in the order Ericales, of which about 50 genera and more than 400 species are restricted to tropical and subtropical areas. They are mostly small to medium-height plants, with simple leaves and small flowers. They are usually shade-loving (foliose), and usually herbs.

The genus Hydrangea has become an invasive species in parts of North America, where it competes with native plants.

About the name

The name "Aquifoliaceae" was established in 1816 by Christiaan Hendrik Persoon, who was a student under Alexander Braun and Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, and who was later a professor at the University of Göttingen in Germany and a co-founder of the science of systematics of living organisms. The word "aquifoliaceae" is an amalgamation of the Latinized forms of Aquilapollenites, a genus of fern allies described during the time of C.H.Persoon, and Hydrangea, a genus for which he did botanical investigations. Bangalore, India / December 19, 2016 / A recent case highlighted the potential perils of trying to bring in a third party to take financial responsibility for a company's debts. A former chairman of a company now facing a winding up order filed an insolvency petition against his erstwhile employer, claiming that it was his efforts that saw the company become profitable. The former chairman also filed a petition against a third party, requesting that it accept liability for the debts of the entity. However, the High Court rejected his petition.
The petitioner had founded a company in 1982 to manufacture and supply components to major multinational auto manufacturers. However, the company fell into financial difficulties within a year of establishment. The company was dissolved in 1988 after an order of winding up was issued by the Karnataka High Court.
Subsequently, the petitioner was reappointed as chairman for a few months between 1999 and 2000. Under his leadership, the company's fortunes changed markedly - it reported profits of over ₹ 1 crore in 1997, ₹ 80 lakhs in 1998 and ₹ 1.6 crore in 1999. A new season brings new opportunities — a chance to get back on track, to meet fresh people and to put a better foot forward than in the previous twelve months. Keep the momentum going and ask people about the work that they have been up to, or to arrange meetings and get togethers. The role of Greek banks in Greece’s economic crisis – the case of Alpha Bank
Athanasios Tavros
This paper seeks to analyse the role of the Greek banking sector during the crisis, and investigates the reasons for the sharp deterioration of the quality of Greek banking supervision and supervision and supervision mechanisms during the Greek crisis. Particular attention is given to the behaviour of the Alpha Bank Group (‘Group’ or ‘Bank’) which was in fact the first domestic Greek investment bank to face capital market pressure from the European Union. The aim is to assess the quality of supervision provided and the actions taken by the Greek Supervisory Authorities (‘SAs’), focusing particularly on the role of Alpha Bank, the first commercial bank to face capital market pressure from the European Union.
Keywords: Greece; banking; Alpha Bank; capital markets
The article will use the Greek financial and banking crisis of 2010-mid-2014 as an example to assess the role of domestic European commercial banks, in particular, in relation to the development and implementation of the banking union in Europe, which aimed to increase financial stability through cross-border supervision.

It’s true that the world is changing faster than it has in the history of humanity. And the changes are more fundamental.
At the end of the 20th century most of the world’s economic activity was generated within national boundaries through national economic planning, through national infrastructure and national investment in factories, roads and people. Today, the majority of economic activity is generated through the operation of a single global enterprise, the largest single entity the world has ever experienced.
The power is shifting from the nation state toward the multi-national corporation, as national governments have become more and more dependent on the operation of large scale commercial enterprises.
That shift from the nation state to a global super-state, with multinational corporations at its heart, has been ongoing for the last 30 years. There is, however, the strong possibility that the global super-state is about to collapse, because of the collapse in consumer spending caused by the banking industry.
This is because banks create money out of thin air by making loans, but they are also a major component of the global super-state, because banking transactions generate massive numbers of paper dollars or electronic dollars, which are traded globally through the interlinked banking industry. A team of researchers led by Professor Catherine Hankins in the John Curtin School of Medical Research (JCSMR) and UNSW Scientia Fellow Dr Saman Bemrah is collaborating to trial a new drug regimen for the treatment of HIV. Professor Katherine Kedzierska from the Telethon Kids Institute in Perth will help test the safety of long-term therapy with the promising antiretroviral drug bictegravir.
The drug, which has just been approved for use in Western Australia on compassionate grounds, is the latest of a series of antiretroviral drugs that block the activity of HIV (the virus that causes AIDS), and is being trialled in patients with long-term HIV infection.
HIV has become a chronic disease in Australia. More than 100,000 people living with the virus are receiving HIV care in this country, and there are over 4,300 children growing up with HIV. The majority of Australians living with HIV can now expect a normal lifespan because of highly effective antiretroviral prevention and treatment regimens.

A lot of our customers get asked a lot of questions regarding "how much is enough" in regard to hiring their first personal trainer. It's really a simple question that I've struggled with and it's one that I'm still grappling with. I always tell people to figure out what they really like doing and really try and enjoy doing it. In terms of exercise, people tend to gravitate towards certain activities. They might love lifting weights or doing HIIT workouts. Or they might like swimming, biking, or running. They might love strength training, yoga or barre. Figure out your interests and your style and then seek out personal trainers whose activities match your personality and fitness goals. Most personal trainers will be able to provide some free training sessions as part of finding a suitable fitness plan.
The second most important question I get asked is "do you believe in personal trainers". The answer to that is an absolute yes. Personal trainers are very valuable. The National Health Commission, China’s top authority in charge of health affairs, published a guideline on the clinical diagnosis of COVID–19 last month, after several months of development. The guideline sets out a classification system for COVID-19, a more detailed diagnosis process from chest CT, and the criteria that define a case as a COVID–19 case under the new diagnosis system. The case definition published in the guideline is based on the case definition published by the World Health Organization (WHO) in May.

According to the first-generation case definition published by WHO, the criteria for detecting COVID-19 include fever, fatigue, and lung changes shown on chest CT. These criteria, however, are not always applicable in some clinical situations. The National Health Commission has therefore modified the criteria and improved the diagnosis process. The definition includes three stages: clinical diagnosis, CT diagnosis, and laboratory diagnosis. The new criteria require two or more of the following six clinical manifestations in the three preceding days.

 Fever
 Fever that lasts for more than three days or with a body temperature higher than 37.3 ℃
 Fatigue
 Fatigue that lasts for more than three days
 Dyspnea
 Dyspnea with breathlessness that lasts for more

The Church’s battle against evil is often a battle to keep up appearances, and to ensure that her enemies are on their guard. It is a battle to be powerful and keep one’s enemies within sight. Hedgehog is a popular species of mammal in southern parts of United Kingdom, due to which it is popularly known as Common Hedgehog or English Hedgehog. It belongs to Erinaceidae family, and like other species of hogs, it is nocturnal. Hedgehog is a popular companion animal choice due to the fact they don’t cause allergy or other discomfort to pets that share their homes.
The most commonly found hedgehogs in homes are the European Hedgehog or European Porcupine which is widely kept for its intelligence and sociability. Other species native to Great Britain are the Short-tailed Hedgehog, Montpellier Hedgehog and the Sardinian Hedgehog or Satin Hedgehog, a tiny species that is usually relict to the former.
Hundreds of thousands of hedgehogs are found as pets, and the market of Hedgehog food continues to expand. They are great pets, and do well when you take proper care of them.
They have been in existence for millions of years, and the earliest fossil evidence of hedgehogs being alive was 3 million years old. There are different species of hedgehogs, and each species has different size, diet

Home › News & Media › Business News › Business Confidence in Manufacturing Industry at Seven Year High
The Manufacturing Barometer, published by Trend Research Institute Ltd, has recorded the highest business confidence since 2008 for Slovenia's primary industries. More than half of respondents are convinced that economic and financial conditions in their primary industry will improve in the future, and the number of survey respondents who believe such conditions will deteriorate has decreased to an all-time low.
Most survey respondents in the manufacturing industry expect the domestic business environment to remain or improve (44.6%) while only a small percentage expect the country's business activity to fall (19.2%). The highest proportion of respondents is convinced that global economic and financial conditions will improve (50.3%) followed by those who expect them to deteriorate (43.6%).
The lowest levels of confidence on Europe's and the world's economies and the global financial market were recorded following the Brexit referendum, at 29.0% and 20.7% respectively, while in September and October of this year, a significant increase of 41.0% and 40.0% in overall confidence was recorded respectively.

Lebanon: The CEDRE conference
A year after the CEDRE conference, Lebanon has yet to implement any of the agreements that were reached.
At the CEDRE conference on April 6, 2017, donors pledged to the Lebanese government $11.5bn in loan and grant support over the next 10 years to help improve basic services, promote economic growth and increase employment in Lebanon, in exchange for the Lebanese government to undertake long-overdue reform measures.
The Lebanese government welcomed the CEDRE support as “critical” to meeting the country’s needs, saying that the support came at the “right time”.
The conference was held in the aftermath of the massive demonstrations which rocked Lebanon throughout the winter of 2016, with demonstrators rallying in the streets for months before a security crackdown on protesters. The demonstrations led to the resignation of then-Prime Minister Michel Aoun and President Michel Suleiman.
But since the CEDRE conference, the Lebanese government has failed to implement a single policy that was agreed upon.
For example, in May 2018 the government approved the CEDRE draft finance bill. But on the first working day, it withdrew the bill.
In the following months, the government postponed the bill

A new report by the World Bank has outlined the potential of e-commerce and digital business in the Kingdom, with a focus on trade facilitation, and highlights that if action is not taken, these avenues to growth are easily lost.
The report, titled ‘Boosting Digital Business: Transforming Trade and Investment in the Middle East and North Africa’, is a joint effort between the World Bank and Dubai Chamber. A key finding is that digital transformation of trade and business in the region requires not just more digital connections, connectivity costs and infrastructure, but also an enabling policy and regulatory environment, better understanding of the role of e-commerce, and education and training of workers and entrepreneurs.
A lack of regional and regional connectivity, especially in the trade-reliant GCC, will significantly impede economic productivity, competitiveness and job creation. Digital platforms can help to reduce barriers to trade and reduce the cost of doing business but progress needs to be made on the latter two aspects. This is especially important since the Middle East and North Africa have been slow to benefit from the rise of e-commerce, with many countries lagging behind other global regions in digital transformation and online sales penetration, according to the report.

This is the second volume of the complete chronology of the life of Emperor Napoleon. It covers the ten years of the First Empire (1804-14). To this day, it remains unrivalled in its detail. This volume covers the period of the Consulate (1804-09) and the years of the Empire (1809-14). It opens with the final days of the Peace of Amiens (March 1802- August 1803) when Napoleon Bonaparte returned to his imperial glory, a feat achieved by force of arms (1805-1810) and the creation of an empire in Western Europe. It is followed by a summary of the Congress of Vienna (1814-15) in the final years of the Empire. Each year is broken down into months, with events covered by the most important dates in the European and Napoleonic calendar with notes describing key battles, campaigns, court life and personalities of the day. This book contains all the elements for a complete understanding of the time, a true masterpiece of scholarship that will last for over two centuries

A new survey out in Germany finds that people are more likely to use the Internet if they are given rewards or are paid to access certain sites. If a site's been used for a while, and people have become comfortable using it, then paying them may even deter them rather than attract them, even in Germany.
But in the U.S. and Canada, the survey found, people use the Internet almost exclusively due to free usage. And, in both those countries, the Web is just a means to get entertainment.
Nearly six hours, or 41 percent, of Americans' Web time is spent listening to broadcast television shows and movies through the Internet. More than 40 percent of German Web users listen to radio and television shows on the Internet.
Ads for "online" products are the fourth-highest-rated type of commercial on the Internet. About 70 percent of German Web users see ads, while 80 percent of Canadian ones do so. About 60 percent of Americans do.
The Internet has become a key tool in managing money in Canada where online banking is far more commonly used than in the U.S. More than 50 percent of Canadians said they had opened an online savings account versus 22 percent of Americans.
