What is a BIP?
BIPs are used to improve the quality of an image. This is done by applying the best possible techniques to that specific area only. The techniques used for the restoration can then be applied on the original image without altering it.
This means you can still edit the untouched image later on, but it will have a “befores” and “afters”, so you can see how much work has gone into it.
What does it matter? I can always do another restoration. I want to preserve the original image.
That is true, but once you make an original print, you will never get it the same way again (just try making a digital copy on a computer and see how it goes). You will lose quality, and it will always seem like a copy, despite your best efforts.
So it is better to print copies of your work, as opposed to making them. The BIP can be printed, and the quality will remain exactly the same!
Why use a digital restoration?

Vicodin: Everything You Need To Know About Vicodin
In the United States, about 20% of the population takes medications for different health conditions such as chronic pain, anxiety, insomnia and others. Many of those people use painkillers often referred to as opioids. One of the most commonly used painkiller in a US market is Vicodin, and it’s being used to treat pain caused by injuries, surgery, cancer, and chronic diseases like Crohn’s or arthritis.
But just because they come with such a good reputation doesn’t mean they don’t carry their own set of hazards and can cause dependency if used too frequently. This article is going to take a look at the addictive properties of Vicodin and give you the facts.
What is Vicodin?

Hydrocodone works as a sedative by changing the chemicals and transmitting them into the brain. Acetaminophen is an anti-inflammatory agent that also reduces fever and pain.

The NAB’s TV Licence Amendment Bill has finally been published and can be found online. A key proposed change that would require those who want to watch and record free-to-air television to buy a new type of ‘personal use only’ licence when they have been given the option to continue to access television shows using the traditional licence model.
The new Licence Type will be called a “TV User Licence” and will allow for “personal use only with the exception of time-shifted viewing and recorded TV programmes”. It will allow access to a limited range of free-to-air programmes available within one’s coverage area.
The NAB has now given notice in its submission on the Bill (available to download below this article) to have this category of licence removed, arguing that it does not represent a true and meaningful distinction and would “fail to provide the level of consumer choice that should be offered”.
The industry body has also called to restrict the new category of licence to those who are no longer watching free-to-air television. If the changes to the licensing model are made and these views were accepted, the NAB says that it could see a 50% reduction in the NAB’s existing licence fee payers

The Battle of Waterloo was mainly fought between France and the Seventh Coalition, led by Great Britain at the height of the Napoleonic Wars. After the previous battles (including the French Invasion of Russia), Napoleon was forced to retreat. The Waterloo Campaign fought in Belgium in 1815, was the climactic battle of the Napoleonic Wars.
Battle of Waterloo.
The Battle of Waterloo was mainly fought between France and the Seventh Coalition, which was led by Britain and its allies. The battle began on 15 and lasted until 18 June 1815. Napoleon Bonaparte, the deposed French emperor, commanded the army, and his opponents were led by the Duke of Wellington.
The Napoleonic Wars had lasted for over a quarter of a century and had involved most of the major powers in Europe, with battles fought from Portugal to Russia

For me, the music of the decade which will forever remain my favourite is the music of the early 80s – the albums I adored and listened to over and over again were a mix of my parents’ old records, my brother’s albums and some of the biggest and best from the charts of the time.

It is almost always a good idea to work from a backup of any system data. There are multiple backup and restore functions on computers that can come in the form of standard, system-wide, software (e.g. Windows) or hardware (e.g. a thumb drive). Some software, such as Microsoft OneNote®, is designed to run entirely off a computer drive and save all of the data directly to the hard drive – but if the computer hard drive is lost or broken, you still have your backups to rely on.
Some backup software only records changes to files - while this can be sufficient for personal use, for business systems, including Microsoft Office or Google Documents, you generally want incremental backups, which include the full history of a file - whether it has been updated, not updated, or deleted. Incremental backups can be created with software on personal machines and computers. Incremental backups are less costly, and are more reliable when more than one machine is used.
To keep your files as up to date as possible without needing a backup, consider using Microsoft OneDrive® cloud document storage, which allows you to open, edit, save, and share documents from anywhere.
A good backup solution is worth the cost and

The outlook for Tuesday will be for a little more of the same, with temperatures possibly getting into the low triple-digits by midafternoon in our area. Tuesday night and Wednesday are similar to the last few days, as well, with high temperatures in the middle to upper 80s. A friend pointed me towards this recent article by Scott McLemee titled “The Problem with Purity: On the Triumph of Cultural Marxism in the English Department“. His premise is that, in my mind, the problem of PC culture is caused by Marxist theory having dominated the English PhD programs since the 1970s, and that the decline of English is mirrored in the decline of Western Civilization.
This seems a bit of a red herring to me. The problem with “Marxist theory” is not that it has become too popular; rather, it has become a tool of the politically correct, and a tool they use to attack their enemies.
McLemee says that a professor he knew, who was a student in the late 1970s and early 1980s, told him that “most of the books and theories I learned about in school didn’t pass the scrutiny of an actual reader after a few years of teaching.” He says that this is true even by “the generally tolerant and liberal standards of the English Department.”

This book is very interesting. The descriptions of the horrors of life in the trenches, the dangers of war in the air and the brutality and hatred of the German troops is vivid. This book will not appeal to everyone. Those who like historical fiction would probably prefer to read a novel with more action. Those who enjoy history would want a book with more detail. I liked it. I did not think it was too long, I was not overwhelmed with detail. It kept me turning pages. The Best Time To Visit New Orleans
New Orleans Hotels and Vacation Packages
New Orleans is a year-round destination with a temperate climate featuring the most mild winters you can find in the U.S.
As with most of the southern U.S., hot temperatures reach 90 degrees Fahrenheit in New Orleans throughout the summer, whereas most central U.S. cities top 100 degrees during that time.
New Orleans is most comfortable during the months of January and June when temperatures average 60-70 degrees. In the fall, November through February, New Orleans averages 70-75 degrees, perfect for exploring outdoor attractions such as the city’s lush Garden District and its beautiful, tree-lined avenues.
The city also boasts plenty of festivals every day of the year that attract millions of visitors from all over the world.
New Orleans' best weather season takes place during its spring (March-May), as well as mid-fall (October and November).
New Orleans has a wet yet mild climate, and the highest chance for rain occurs from October through February.

Hive Hub's second major investment announced just two months after opening
A new business centre in Stoke is to receive almost half a million pounds from the UK Government, as part of a £2 million investment in the region.
Hive Hub is a serviced office operator, which has created a space in Hanley for businesses to work in.
It opened the doors in September last year and has already gained 40 companies with more lined up.
Now the firm is gearing up for a second new office, which will be an extension of the one it has in Stoke.
It's hoped this will make it the biggest office offering in the area with the capacity to create up to 100 new jobs.
Hive Hub's Paul Millington said: “This £430,000 investment in the new Stoke centre represents a huge vote of confidence from the UK Government.
“We are proud to be a growing part of the regional and national economy which will help build the digital economy, attract inward investment and stimulate the local economy.”

Michael "Mike" George Phelan
Michael “Mike” George Phelan “Mike”, age 72, passed away peacefully on Saturday, December 28, 2019, with his four children by his side at the Bethany Care Center at the age of 72.
Mike was born to the parents of the late, James George and Mary Ann (McGuire) Phelan on October 24, 1947 in Worcester.
Mike was a lifelong resident of Worcester. He graduated from St. John’s High School. He served his country with the US Navy.
Mike was a hard and honest worker. He most recently worked for the Department of Conservation. His work ethic was unmatched by anyone who knew him.

It has been estimated that each year some 15,000,000 bushels of grain are lost in the harvest of the United States. In addition, the loss of grain in storage from the action of microorganisms, insect, and rodents, may be as high as 18,000,000 bushels annually.

A new book on the art of living, all about how to connect
Families have a shared DNA.
This is the belief of the family researchers we talk to. They are on to something important, about human nature and the family. In their world, the most important thing humans do is connect. The way in which humans connect affects the health and well-being of all generations. And this happens most fundamentally, the researchers say, within families.
This week on our podcast we talk to two of these researchers – Professor Bruce Link from Carnegie Mellon University and Dr. Daniel Amen from Amen Clinic.
Professor Link is a family psychiatrist working in social science. He studies the ways in which families make decisions that affect their health and wellbeing over generations. His most recent research has focused on the effects of family on the brain and mental health.
Dr. Amen’s clinic specializes in brain health and mental wellness. He studies and treats brains and mental health in a way that is often counter to traditional treatment. He treats every brain – with every type of brain disorder. Every family in that makes up a person’s network. Every type of person, no matter how complicated their story.
For Bruce Link, and Daniel Amen, families

Newly-discovered 'alien' planet orbits star twice the size of Jupiter
Scientists have discovered an alien world so far away it takes 12,000 years to orbit its star - and the planet is twice the size of Jupiter.
By John Bett
10:24AM BST 04 Aug 2014
J1407b, which was discovered over the weekend as part of a larger study into exoplanets, is 10 per cent larger than Jupiter and located 1,200 light years away from Earth.
The planet is so far away, it takes a whopping 12,000 years to orbit its star, which is almost three times the average lifetime of a star in our galaxy.
The planet is almost completely covered in water and orbits a star twice its size, according to astronomers from the National Astronomical Observatory of Japan.
It is made entirely of water, which means J1407b is more similar to Europa, a moon made up of water and ice in Jupiter's orbit.
Astronomers have discovered a planet so remote it may never be seen, which revolves around a star two and a half times as big as the Sun.

Osteoporosis is a disease in which the bones lose density and weaken. Bone loss occurs from a combination of factors, including bone density, bone turnover and bone metabolism, according to the National Institutes of Health (NIH). The NIH says that some lifestyle choices can help prevent the onset or progression of osteoporosis.
The NIH lists the following activities as being able to reduce the risk of osteoporosis: not smoking, limiting alcohol consumption, limiting caffeine intake, maintaining a healthy body weight, exercising and being physically active every day, eating a balanced diet, limiting salt intake and getting regular intake of calcium, vitamin D and vitamin D-fortified products, especially for women, as recommended by the NIH.
Osteoporosis, according to the NIH, can also be prevented through the use of hormone therapy. The NIH notes that hormones are needed to maintain bone strength during the growth period and that women are at particular risk during menopause. Hormone replacement therapy can help maintain bone health in women. The NIH says the therapy may be beneficial for some women taking glucocorticoids or those with an adrenal disorder, either of which can cause bone loss.

A few weeks ago, the National Weather Service (NWS) held an annual workshop in conjunction with the Hurricane Center (Hurricane Center of Excellence) in Miami, Florida. I was fortunate to be there, and I got a chance to meet a few of the great folks that make up the Hurricane Center.
For those of us that are just beginning to work with storm surge, it was a great chance to learn from the experts, and it is a subject so often in our headlines that having access to people who know and understand storm surge is vital for our mission efforts.
During the presentations, a few presentations really stood out to me. The first of these was presented by Dr. Chris Landsea (of NASA), who showed the current understanding of the role storm surge plays in tropical cyclone hazards. As Dr. Landsea stated, “storms are complex systems and understanding hurricane hazards is as complex as understanding and describing them”.
If you are looking for a primer on storm surge, I would suggest you check out this post and watch Dr. Landsea’s presentation.

Toward a “World without a Border”
International Border Crossings in Time of Coronavirus
In the midst of the COVID-19 pandemic, many people’s attention and mental health are being impacted by border restrictions, quarantines, and border patrols—as if the walls already in place were not enough. In the United States alone, more than one hundred seventy-five countries can be reached by land and air ports of entry. At any given time, more than two million people cross the border with Mexico, most of them without incident, and millions more people cross the border with Canada as well. However, people seeking asylum at the border with Mexico, and the people who cross it without permission to work, are often deported from the United States within hours of entering their country of origin.
But the border crossing between the United States and Canada are still open. On March 16, 2020, after the United States declared a national emergency on COVID-19, Canada closed the border to nonessential travel, yet it remained open for essential travel such as cross-border trade and supply chains, and also remains open for asylum seekers. We all experience times where we just feel like things aren’t going our way and our lives get a little out of control. We have moments when we feel a little overwhelmed and a little insecure, maybe even lonely, but sometimes we think we might be the only ones who feel like that.
This might be one way of describing an experience that you’ve had, and maybe it’s something you’ve heard others talk about as well. Or maybe it’s a way of describing that experience and a way that we can think about that experience. There’s some really profound ways of thinking about the experience of feeling alone, and for me this has been helpful for dealing with loneliness when I felt like I thought I was the only one in the world experiencing what I was going through.
One of the ways of thinking about the way the experience of feeling alone might affect the way we act is that we might tend to think that the only way we can survive the experience of loneliness is to get some company, some friends to hang out with, someone to share our experience. This paper is a study from the series "Biological Invasions: Economic and Societal Impacts," edited by William R. Smith, Jr. and Janice M. Fullerton. Biological invasions can provide many benefits to the areas in which they occur; however, they may also lead to negative effects in other areas and on other species. The impacts of biological invasions are dependent on a variety of factors, including the life cycle of the species, the location's natural resources, and social and political factors. For this paper, the impacts of biological invasions are studied in three areas: economics, health, and social. Impacts described are for invasive species with limited dispersal capabilities and for the areas in which they are native.

MOSCOW, Nov 2 (Reuters) - Russian authorities said on Wednesday they have opened a money laundering investigation against an anti-corruption campaigner who said she had a suitcase with up to 5 billion rubles ($75.6 mln) in cash stolen from her.
The lawyer representing Elena Khusnitdinova’s daughter said the probe was launched because Khusnitdinova had appealed to President Vladimir Putin for help.
“The reason for the decision is the fact that the investigation agency received an appeal from the family of the activist, alleging that the activist’s daughter has been subjected to physical and psychological pressure, including the fact of putting pressure on the daughter by means of her mother’s appeal to the head of state,” a spokeswoman for Russia’s Investigative Committee said.
Investigative Committee spokeswoman Svetlana Petrenko said the complaint did not explain, but rather “gave an account of the fact that the mother is a money launderer”.
Khusnitdinova, a lawyer for the local environmental NGO, Ecoclub, said on Nov. 1 that a man broke into her flat in a building in central Moscow where she rents an apartment on the sixth floor.

It's a great day in New York when Jonathan Franzen's newest novel opens on a flight to London. Before their flight takes off, two strangers in the same row are chatting. When his seatmate asks him what he does and he replies, "I run a charity that supports poor children," the man sitting next to him leans over and says, "I would love to tell you my story. Vintage photos and memories of old-time Hollywood
Stars of the Star-making "King of the Movies"
by Dick Bartlett III
A rare color photo from the Paramount "King of the Movies" (1932) film release campaign, featuring Marlene Dietrich, Bette Davis and Mae West, among others.
For many of Hollywood’s earliest stars, the movies were a way out of difficult times – not exactly as it seems today, but a step off what was often a painful childhood.
Often their parents didn’t approve of the film careers and sent them elsewhere – or away home, to a parent or relative. That way those early movie experiences of such stars as Joan Crawford, Bette Davis, Judy Garland, Marlene Dietrich, Carole Lombard, Bette Midler, Gloria Swanson and so many others, wouldn’t be as painful, because they’ve been with them all the time. They were even a source of support for the performers, during good and bad times.

The MBA Degree
The MBA (Master of Business Administration) degree was created in the United States and this was the only country in which this degree became widely accepted. Currently, there are more than 845 colleges and universities offering MBAs in the United States.
There are many MBA graduates across all industry sectors with over three million MBA alumni living in the United States. The business world needs business professionals who have the skills to run, manage and analyze large businesses. For these reasons, this degree is a popular choice for individuals.
Many applicants have chosen to pursue their MBA because it has a competitive reputation among potential employers in the United States and worldwide. The average annual salary in the United States for these graduates is about $72,500.

Home / All / Landscapes / 2010 / The Old Bridge, Newtown, Edinburgh - 3 October 2010
In this image of The Old Bridge, Newtown, Edinburgh, Scotland (2010), the sky is lit with a mix of sunset and dawn colours. The Old Bridge (also called South Bridge) was built in 1785 over South Bridge, a major route in and out of Edinburgh's Old Town. Built by Thomas Telford & Co and John Burns, the bridge is one of the oldest continuously-functioning bridges in Edinburgh. Designed by John Hamilton and Thomas Hamilton, the South Bridge was the first bridge ever built in Edinburgh, and it was built in just 17 months. The bridge was damaged twice by floods and finally replaced in 1850 but is still considered to be the first'modern' bridge in the city and is also regarded as a forerunner of modern bridges in general. By the time the bridge was built, the city of Edinburgh was already well-renowned for its beauty and architecture. A number of other bridges were to follow, among which the Balustrade Bridge and the Calton Pedestrian Bridge across the Firth of Forth, although both are now defunct.

Hobbs’s American Medical Gazette and Journal of Health was the first medical journal to be launched in the United States, and it was also one of the first to be printed in color. It ran with an issue frequency of four times per year on a quarterly publication cycle. Founded in 1869, it was initially edited by A. P. Peabody, and then, when it merged with the Chicago Medical Review in 1871, the two were combined under the editorhip of Henry H. Rogers. The Medical Review was founded in 1860, and became the official journal for the Illinois State Medical Society, the Illinois State Academy of Family Physicians, and the Chicago Medical Society.
Hobbs’s American Medical Gazette and Journal of Health, a four times per year periodical, had a total of twenty-four volumes published from 1869 to 1898.
This digital collection includes one volume of the medical journal from 1871. The full contents of the run are not available in HathiTrust due to copyright restrictions.
This page contains links to volumes on HathiTrust which may be freely downloaded, but are still listed under the institution as being restricted due to copyright.

Nashville has a special place in Country Music history with its beginnings as Hatchie Holler and its evolution into America’s Music Capital. The city is a must for those who seek genuine culture, history, and an appreciation for the life and legacy of Jim and Charlie McCoy. The Jim & Charlie McCoy Museum is on a mission to preserve and promote the life and legacy of America’s most beloved and respected bluegrass musicians. This facility honors Jim and Charlie and all who helped put Nashville on the map as a bluegrass player’s town.
The museum has a vast and valuable collection of music memorabilia, historical photographs and many of Jim’s instruments, including his famous Gibson Hummingbird that Jim used to play “My Happiness” so well. The room where Charlie recorded his greatest hits, such as “Country Boy” and “The Great Speckled Bird” is recreated in the museum.
The museum is open on Friday, Saturday, and Saturday evening in June, July and August during the summer. The museum closes annually for the Christmas holiday.

A former U.S. senator from South Dakota, James Abourezk, was recently interviewed on KVEL Radio in Reno, Nevada where he discussed his thoughts on the Iraq war and the impact of the war on American citizens.
Abourezk served in the U.S. House of Representatives and the U.S. Senate before resigning from office in 1978 to go to work for the National Endowment for the Humanities. He has been a vocal opponent of the Iraq War from its inception, even before President Bush took office.

The new album from The Hoots & Hellmouth, a five-piece, old-west inspired rock’n’roll group from the frozen wastelands of Minneapolis, Minn., is a thing of wonder. To get the feeling, try to imagine what The Pogues would sound like if they hung out with Tom Waits a lot, then got lost on a moonshine run through the deep dark woods. Nope, that’s still not what it sounds like. But it’s a fair stab.
Apt comparisons, yes, and not entirely wrong, but it’s missing the point. The most important aspect of The Hoots & Hellmouth isn’t their sound, however endearingly rough, weird and unique it is. There are plenty of those bands, no doubt. What distinguishes this group is its utterly and completely unselfconscious embrace of musical styles and influences so eclectic you’d be hard-pressed to name a common thread.

Crisis management is a skill that must be continually sharpened. All companies, regardless of size or location, should consider having an emergency action plan for potential crisis situations. In addition to creating this plan, it’s also a good idea to develop crisis communication plan to effectively manage a crisis situation in the event of a fire, tornado or any number of situations.
Emergencies can strike at any time and it’s up to everyone from the CEO to administrative staff members to work as a team during these stressful circumstances. A crisis situation can take place over the course of a week, or it can be a more sudden emergency that leaves no time to adequately prepare. In all cases, it is crucial that the company have a plan in the event of an emergency situation. Employees must be able to work as a team and respond to a crisis situation as efficiently and appropriately as possible.
To avoid confusion, it is best to include everyone who will be effected by a crisis situation when creating the crisis communication plan. All individuals involved should understand how to respond to a crisis and the specific details of that plan should the need arise.

My husband and I wanted to take advantage of the beautiful weather and drive to Big Sur to hike Julia Pfeiffer Burns State Beach.
Our drive down the PCH was scenic. We stopped on several locations to take some pictures. We were not too surprised by a few of the stops, but we were in awe at places we did not expect!
As we climbed the hill to Bixby bridge, I was stunned by the redwood trees, we were literally at the bottom of giant redwood trees! The sun was low enough to get great shots of the redwoods.
We made another stop at Ragged Point. The view of the beach and the point was amazing! There were a few seals on the rocks along side the beach.
We decided to eat lunch in the restaurant on Ragged Point. I was impressed that Ragged Point had an indoor dining area. It was a nice temperature, but it was a bit cramped. We were the only ones dining inside, the outdoor area was already full.
The sunset was spectacular! The red sky was a sight to see. We were able to see this sunset from our car!
We made another stop after our hike, but this time we had the full moon lighting our way!

On the third of November 2017, myself and my wonderful friend, Laura, finally fulfilled a promise we made to each other five years ago: we went to the theatre together!
The play we watched (for £12.50, a quarter, or a little more than $21 Cdn each) was The Glass Menagerie, written by Tennessee Williams, and directed by John Simmit.
John is an incredible actor who plays everything from the lead in the musical Kinky Boots to the villain in Dr Who and has appeared on Strictly Come Dancing.
Laura and I spent so many hours over the years discussing how we should combine our love of theatre with our interest in artistry and my love of photography to create something truly amazing … and The Glass Menagerie was the start of it! We both agreed on a production of The Glass Menagerie by a well-known director, in a theatre where tickets were affordable, in which we’d sit together and watch together, and with which I’d take photos. It was so perfect. And when we found out that John Simmit was directing it, our hearts grew so many sizes.

What is the definition of the term "social justice"?

Define social justice and discuss how economic justice plays out for workers, families, communities, and entire societies?


A:  Social Justice is a humanist political goal. It was conceived of by Karl Marx as part of the Marxist doctrine and was introduced as a general concept in the 19th century in German sociology. The idea of social justice is also one of the core components of the liberal social contract theory.

Marx considered it a humanist political goal because:

1. He saw it as an end to poverty and a means to an end

2. He saw it as a prerequisite to a free and democratic society


Marx thought that justice had been "lost" from society in the feudalism or capitalism era and that a social order that would bring justice back was socialism or communism.

For Marx, justice entailed:

1. Economic equality (in terms of the level of income) among members of society

2. Democratic decision-making in a society

3. The rule of law

4. Freedom

However, these ideas are not universally adhered to by all thinkers in the social justice movement. It is

The RSPB, The Nature Conservancy and The Woodland Trust are joining forces in a bid to bring home the plight of the song thrush.
The RSPB has called for people to write a letter to their MP and ask them to support the All Party Parliamentary Group for Conservation's inquiry into the songbird. They hope this will lead to legislation to protect it.
A song thrush's main threat is the spread of the infectious disease called Leptospirosis. This is carried by rats, mice and voles and affects the bird's kidneys. It has also been caused by poor hygiene.
Song birds face several other challenges, including the destruction of habitat and competition for food with other species such as the house sparrow. The RSPB says this bird has halved in the UK since the 1960s.
The All Party Parliamentary Group for Conservation (APPGC) is inviting submissions to its inquiry on the song thrush. Its chairman is Mark Pritchard MP, vice chairman is Chris Skidmore MP, and vice chair for Natural Environment is Andrew Percy MP.

The next time you feel your chest and your neck and your shoulders ache in the afternoon, the pain may stem from your upper back muscles, which take a lot of work to keep the rest of the body going. The muscles that extend from the spine from the base of the middle back to the top of the shoulder are known as thoracic muscles, or the back of the back. The thoracic muscles do everything from taking care of your neck from being turned to supporting your spine, your hips and your legs.
When you bend to pick up a baby or shovel snow, the muscles in your back are constantly used. But with age, work and other factors, these muscles can sometimes get stiff and tense and then lead to lower back pain. People who have back pain and have thoracic pain often have problems with those two things together, said Dr. John Briffa from the Centre for Human Performance in Dublin, Ireland.
“A lot of people with stiff necks and with stiff shoulders, as well as back pain, actually have a whole lot of back pain with thoracic (pain) as well,” said Dr. Richard Rosenberg from the Institute of Physical Medicine & Rehabilitation in Stamford, Conn. What is the most common type of work accident?
Many cases of occupational accidents are caused in a workplace accident during maintenance of equipment, or in other activities related to work. In these instances, the primary responsibility is not to make a mistake. In many cases these are minor accidents, even small falls and bruising due to falls from height. In other cases, serious accidents can occur with a large loss of life.
In other areas that do not involve a mechanical cause of injury, it can be caused by the way you take care of the work. Often, these cases are due to the lack of training, lack of instructions given and the lack of knowledge of the dangers arising from these working conditions and the associated lack of safety measures.
But whatever the cause, we must all keep in mind that work accidents are the same in principle, and that it is necessary to adopt appropriate measures to protect ourselves from such situations.

Ancestry.com has uploaded the 1835-1839 slave schedules for Maryland and Delaware on their website for free access. This is great news since genealogists have always felt that the original schedules could not be found elsewhere. Unfortunately, Ancestry has provided no clues to the location of the documents, although they do provide a "hint" in some cases. I will be spending some time trying to find these schedules in the coming months. This is a wonderful addition to the records that are now available on Ancestry and will make a great resource for anyone researching ancestors who may have owned slaves in the Chesapeake region.
My goal in this article is to provide the first in a series of posts on how to research enslaved African Americans and their families in Maryland and Delaware. A second post will examine specific topics related to those documents, such as the identification of slaveholders, slaves, and other members of the enslaved family units. I am starting with a brief overview of both states since many of the records I will discuss were not created until after 1835. Many of those records are also held by the Maryland Historical Society and the Historical Society of Delaware, and will be examined in other posts.

The next big trend in computing?
This year's Consumer Electronics Show introduced a lot of cool new gadgets. But one trend that's been around for a while is still showing promise – in-memory computing. The technology's in its infancy but it could be the game changing technology needed to accelerate application performance when it's the bottleneck – especially in cloud environments.
In-memory computing uses main memory to store data, as opposed to other types of storage like hard drives or solid state drives. Data that's stored in main memory has access to all available memory at all times. When data is stored outside of main memory, only the memory that the application currently needs is used, creating what's known as RAM bottlenecks. While it's more efficient to store data on hard drives or solid state disks, when you need more memory, accessing hard drives is much slower than accessing memory.
That brings us to the promise of in-memory computing. In addition to storing data in main memory, an application can also process data before writing it to disk, effectively accelerating performance.

Lisandro Martínez (2 June 1844 – 31 January 1923) was an Argentine military officer who rose to the rank of General during his career. He served as Minister of War and Minister of Foreign Affairs.

In 1868, Martínez joined the army and quickly was promoted to major. He fought in the War of the Triple Alliance. He was commander of the 1st Brigade of the Argentine Army in the War of the Triple Alliance (1868-1870, in which he took part in the Battle of Pavón and the surrender ceremony to General Parodi in Santiago del Estero).

Martínez remained in the military until his retirement in 1916.

Martínez participated in his last public duty as Minister of Foreign Affairs on January 9, 1913, at the opening of the new residence of the Argentine ambassador in the German capital Berlin before the departure of Governor Mariano R. Pedraza to Germany with the objective of resolving the problem of water for the Argentine provinces of Santa Fe and Buenos Aires.

The Life and Times of Little Tich
A biography of Leonard Douglas Verrall
Leonard Douglas Verrall
Subjects: Biography, Dance
Series: PMLA Monograph Series
Pages: 248 Size: 6.125 x 9.25
Illustrations: 35 black-and-white illustrations, 2 tables
This book, a biography of Leonard Douglas Verrall, traces his career as a dancer, actor, and writer under the pseudonym Thomas Little. It examines both his career as an actor on the London stage and his career as a dancer with John Martin’s American company and with the Royal Italian Opera House. Drawing from interviews with former company members, the book offers a colorful picture of life as a member of the company, and a more complete picture of Verrall’s varied career as an actor and as a dancer. The author also offers a new biographical sketch of Verrall and an annotated index listing the performances he staged, the other theaters he worked at, his published works, and a list of his principal friends.
This book is a valuable contribution to the study of early twentieth-century entertainment.

The Bizarre World of Kitsch and the Importance of Keeping a Sense of Humor
Kitsch is defined as objects or practices considered “tacky, cheesy, or sentimental.” It is often characterized as something made on a large scale in commercial quantities and meant to appeal to the masses. When we describe a movie, for example, as “dopey, silly, corny, or cliché,” we are referring to it as kitsch.
We live in a world saturated with kitsch. Everything we buy is kitsch to some degree — from the toothpaste to the refrigerator to the car. Sometimes, however, even the smallest things in life can become kitschy, and the most obvious example is the internet. A simple Google search will show that the internet is inundated with the kitschiest of kitsch.

Posted at Feb 19 2019 10:03 PM
The Greek coastguard pulls a migrant from a rubber dinghy that sank on its way to the Greek island of Rhodes, some 80 kilometres away, near the town of Skala Sidi Limni in the Greek port of Kos. IKATERINI GAIZA, AFP
ATHENS - The winter storms hammering weather-beaten northern Europe are wreaking fresh havoc for migrants stuck in limbo on Aegean Sea islands off Turkey, as authorities struggle to cope with record numbers of arrivals.
On Kos, a Greek island popular with Britons with several resorts, some 450 migrants were awaiting rescue late Monday. A second boat carrying about 30 people was spotted in bad weather.
About 700 people -- including 200 women, children and minors -- had arrived on the island from nearby Turkey over the previous nine days, the mayor told AFP.
On Samos, a small island a few kilometres to the east, about 120 people were sheltering after being picked up by search and rescue crews.

The Eames Lounge Chair is one of the most recognised designs of all time. Created in 1956 by Charles and Ray Eames, it has been manufactured for over 40 years in a simple yet elegant design, and remains a firm favourite to this day. A true icon of design and style, the Eames Lounge Chair has stood the test of time to still be relevant and desirable in today’s interior design.
The Eames Lounge Chair was created for Herman Miller by Charles and Ray in 1956, it is recognisable from its elegant lines that make it an essential piece in a modern home. The chair is constructed from bent plywood – a material Charles and Ray Eames often used – with a black vinyl-covered cushions. As well as being a great option when it comes to comfort, it can easily be wiped clean with a duster.

Lorraine and Mary Smith of Loddon Vale Community Garden are hosting ‘The Women Behind the Women’, a celebration of women, their role in communities and their support to help those in need. The event will be held this Wednesday, 9 August, at Loddon Vale Community Garden in Gisborne from 1.30pm to 3pm.
This year marks the 30th anniversary of the National Association of Women’s Councils. It also happens to be National Women’s Shout Out Day and for Lorraine and Mary the day has special significance, as a number of the women coming to the event will be representing the Association. The event will feature a celebration of women by recognising the work and achievements of local women in their community. It will also celebrate National Women’s Shout Out Day, through the voices of the local Loddon women who will address the guests and share their own stories.

The "Piano" is composed of two keyboards, keyboard and bass.
This "Keyboard" or "Piano" has been designed to serve as a musical keyboard or a controller to drive a MIDI or CV/Gate machine. The user can select between one to four different voices, that can be tuned in various ways. When in tune, the musical keyboard is in "tuneful mode". When the user pushes the "tuneful" button, then the keyboard turns into a normal one with only one sound (or voice).
The "Bass" is designed to allow the user to tune the two keyboard voices into a bass and a melody. The "Bass" feature on the keyboard can also be used as a normal piano.
A "Switch" allows the user to switch between the "tuneful mode" and "normal keyboard mode" (or vice versa).
Note the special "tremolo" button. This causes the two keys to vibrate.
Note the "octave" button (the octave button doubles the keyboard pitches).
The "velocity" button allows the user to select which key-press will register as which note.

Prayers for the Dead
by A.L. Kennedy
Published by Fourth Estate
Review by Rachael Acks
Prayers for the Dead is the second novel in a trilogy of mysteries from A.L. Kennedy, set in the dark days between World War One and the Jazz Age. It is a story that, to take just one example, begins with an injured man with no memory of who he is or why he is here. That’s a fairly grim introduction, but it isn’t going to be the first clue to his story. Not long after he arrives in a rural country town, he finds his name on a train schedule with the name of a family he’s never known—family that includes a daughter who’s never heard him mention her.
It’s a classic murder mystery story, with a cast of eccentric characters, and the whole narrative is told third person, from the outside, the way an observer would have to take in the goings on of the town and its inhabitants.

The E-Liquid Vaper was started on June 17, 2012.
The idea for the name came from the fact that the “e” is the eleventh letter of the alphabet and the “vapin'” is the eighth letter. The two come together in a very catchy way with the first five letters making a VEE.

text:
"Unemployment, Relative Price Dispersion and Financial Repression in Africa: Macroeconomic Impact of Remittances"
African Journal of Economic and Sustainable Development
Vol.5 No.13, 2015
DOI: https://doi.org/10.9734/ajesd/2015/27310
Keywords: Africa; remittances; unemployment
This paper explores the impact of remittances on unemployment and relative prices in Africa. This paper focuses mainly on Nigeria and Kenya since both have the largest remittance inflow in sub-Saharan Africa. Using a modified stochastic frontier approach and using monthly data from 1995 to 2014, this paper finds a strong negative impact of remittances on unemployment in these two countries. Furthermore, for all countries in Africa excluding these two countries, remittances have no significant impact on unemployment. However, for Nigeria and Kenya, remittances negatively influences relative prices. These findings imply the importance of ensuring that migrants return to Africa and invest.
Efrem Wichman1
1International Monetary Fund, Umuobiakwem, Onitsha, Anambra State, Nigeria.

text:
A full time vegan, poet, artist and woman born in the cold north but currently based in the very sunny south of France, I live with my beautiful, supportive partners and my doggie son. Although I have suffered from mental health since childhood, I only really came out to myself as an adult. And, as I have come out for myself so I became strong and empowered, and I have never looked back.
I am a strong, loud, proud transwoman who has never once let her trans status define her, but instead used it as the driving force through which I now make my stand for other members of the transgender community. I stand up for their rights and I speak out for them. When I have experienced abuse, discrimination, isolation or just general ignorance, I always stand up and speak out. I no longer have the patience for people thinking they know or understand me, and I have little patience with people who do not stand up for my brothers and sisters and say clearly that they do not want them to be treated without respect, decency, and dignity.
I started writing to record a diary of my journey and how I have changed as a human being, and my experiences as a trans woman. I wrote poems for myself, to give myself strength

text:
Hurrah! A cooperative worldwide effort to rescue civilization! As someone who was involved with the founding of the Internet and World Wide Web, I am pleased to see the effort to build an Internet with enough bandwidth to support the future of civilization as we know it — and then some! A good example of this is the work here to move the IETF to Iceland
By way of background, an early IETF meeting in 1991 occurred in the same room in which this meeting occurs. This meeting was also an Icelandic first for the Internet. It was the meeting where the world wide web protocol was proposed, discussed and agreed, and the Web was born. I remember standing in the same doorway where we had met in 1991, and it felt like yesterday rather than over 30 years ago.
Thank you, Iceland and the people of Iceland, for having the vision and courage to preserve one of the most important places on Earth, the birthplace of modern civilization.

text: After the martyrdom of St. Boniface in 754, the Anglo-Saxons continued to pay homage to him and to commemorate his feasts through their liturgical tradition and literature. Although Anglo-Saxon poetry is little known today, some of the earliest examples, especially those of the second- and third-century writers such as Caedmon and Cynewulf, are closely tied to the hagiographical tradition. This is the case with the martyrology in which Caedmon (c. 680-660) recounts, in hexameters, the martyrdom of the young Irish slave Caedmon, who refused to renounce his Christian faith to avoid pagan sacrifices (lines 3-20 below). He also includes the martyrdoms of the early Irish bishop St. Fursey (lines 20-30) and Cyneweld (lines 40-50) as well as many later Anglo-Saxon martyrs. Cyneweld is an old friend of the poet's: in the Cynewulfian life of St. Felix, Cyneweld is said to have traveled with St. Columba during his evangelising efforts in Scotland in the late sixth or early seventh century. In a small bowl, combine fennel, capers, onion, olive oil, anchovy, paprika and salt to taste.
Pour into a roasting dish and roast for 30 minutes or so at Gas Mark 4, turning once in the process, and remove from the oven when the fennel is soft.
In a large pan, heat the olive oil over a high heat, add pork and brown on all sides.
Deglaze the pan with white wine and reduce by half. Add in butter and cook for a further minute.
Add stock and vegetables to the pork and season with salt and pepper to taste.
Bring stock to the boil whilst you boil the green beans.
Dice the potatoes very finely, and add to the mixture with the corn kernels.
Pour boiling water into the green bean pods when they turn bright green, boil and drain.
Add potatoes and corn kernels to the pork and vegetables and simmer for a further 10-15 minutes, adding the beans to the mixture once cooked, when almost soft.
To serve, take the mixture off the heat and stir through cream.
Divide into bowls and scatter over rocket and fennel tops from the fennel fronds.
Serve with crusty brown bread.

Pankaj Agarwal, Nithin Raju, Lori Laffel, Thomas P. Conrads, William J McManus, and John McManus
To determine the effects of a variety of methodological factors, particularly the effects of the technical reproducibility of the quantification of mRNA transcripts, on measures of mRNA expression in the liver.
A large cohort of healthy men and women (n = 2637) was followed for 5 years. Mean (SD) age was 52.9 (3.5) years. Using real-time reverse-transcriptase polymerase chain reaction, the technical reproducibility of seven measures of mRNA expression was examined.
For each of the seven measures, there was considerable reproducibility across technical repeats, both within and across subjects. Reproducibility of single gene mRNA expression in the liver was greater for transcripts within a region-specific gene cluster (e.g., HNF4A, HNF1B, KLF15) than for single genes. The reproducibility of multiple regions-specific genes (e.g., HNF1B, HNF4A, KLF15) was greater than that of single region-specific genes for eight of 11 cases
prompt_prefix: Meaningful, reliable and valid mRNA expression analyses

The Center for the Study of Culture, Politics and Economics at the University of Texas A&M University has organized "Cultural Globalization: Politics, Markets and the Global Spread of Information, Ideas and Entertainment, " a symposium on September 17-18 to examine the impacts of increasing globalization and information flows on culture. The symposium is an extension of the Center's research program on cultural globalization, a concept to describe the cultural transformation of countries across the globe in terms of cultural production, distribution, consumption, and cultural policies.
Cultural globalization, says John C. Torpey, director of the Center, "has had a significant influence on the arts. It is at the heart of debates about the democratization of culture as well as the privatization of culture; the growth of the international film industry; and the growing importance of cultural diasporas and their impact on the arts. It has also given rise to the globalization of popular culture and the mass market, as well as the growth of global cultural studies."
Speakers at the symposium will represent a variety of sectors of the cultural and academic community. Participants will include:

For the past 5 years, our fashion week charity event has been called The Butterfly Ward, in honour of all the young people that have battled cancer over the last decade. This year we are raising money in order to provide the wards with a new lounge area, where friends and families can sit and support the young people on the ward in an environment that is more comfortable. The garden area is where the children have spent most of their day.
To see young people so brave in the face of a disease which has no cure, to have the strength to fight back, is an incredible thing.

Well it's been a funny few days. On a good note, it's not raining so at 2:30 p.m. today we finally saw some sun! We were walking outside when a young boy came from the direction of his aunt's house. The Arlington County Board plans to vote Saturday afternoon on an agreement between Arlington and its largest employer, Arlington-based The Washington Football Team.
The agreement sets strict spending limits and other rules for the Washington Football Team during football season — such as restrictions on alcohol, on tailgating and even on the use of a team-owned parking garage for fan pick-up and drop-off.
Team attorney Jim Sheehan said the team agreed to those limits, and he described it as “the only way to make sure the fans can go to the game safely and the park can stay open.”
Among the provisions:
* The team must pay the county a $250,000 cleanup fee by March 2021. The team also can not use its parking ramp adjacent to the stadium to host fans or for parking.
* If the county raises the county’s share of stadium tax revenue, the team must contribute, too.

Menara Taming Sari (Melaka Tower)
MALAYSIA, MALACCA - Built in the 18th Century, this tower of Malacca is the tallest historic building in Malaysia and was formerly a place where the King of Malacca kept his eye on ships in the Strait of Malacca.
Menara Taming Sari is the tallest historical monument on the island of Malacca. It is also said to be the oldest structure that survived in Malaysia.
This 70 meter-wide tower of Malacca is located at the center of a Portuguese Square, which is also known as A' Famosa Square and the name Menara Taming Sari. This monument was commissioned by the Parameswara of Malacca, who was a king of Malacca and ruled in 1389-1402.
There are three stories for Menara Taming Sari, which are each 20 meters wide. The first floor has a mosque which was used by the king and his people to listen to religious teachings and for them to pray. It is also used as an assembly hall for people during ceremonies.
Menara Taming Sari was also known as The Tower of Taming Sari for

That Which You Don’t Be Aware Of
From the days of the pyramids of Egypt and before that, the pyramids of Nubia, the history of mankind’s use of mummies as a preservation medium has proven to be very intriguing. Mummification is a multi-stage process that requires several natural elements to be in place in order for the body to be preserved. Mummification was used by the Egyptians, the ancient Mayans, the Incas of South America, the Chinese, the Egyption, Minoan and early cultures of Europe, and in some parts of Africa.
It all starts with dehydration. The mummified person is placed in a large container such as a wine jug, where it is placed in a chamber for the purpose of allowing the water to evaporate from the body. The skin of the body, as well as any gummy remnants or bodily fluids, dry out leaving the internal organs, the internal tissues, and hair intact.
Next, the body is treated to some kind of preserving oil. Oil is considered to be a very efficient way of preserving a mummy's body from decaying in nature. The Egyptians used oils made of fish (a practice carried on to a lesser degree

South Carolina's first LGBTQ Community Center!The Center will serve as a convening place for Greenville's diverse LGBTQ+ community, and also serve the interests of all those whose lives are impacted by LGBTQ+ issues and who support a safe and respectful environment for all people.

Salt Lake City divorce: alimony in Utah
Alimony in Utah must be determined for a specific period of time or until the death or remarriage of the recipient spouse. Alimony is also known as spousal maintenance. In many divorces in Salt Lake City, alimony is a term that refers to spousal support payments made by one former husband or wife to another.
Divorce in Salt Lake City: child support and custody
Whether in Salt Lake City or another part of Utah, the court will also set the terms of financial support for children of the marriage. When a court determines child custody, it is typically given to the parent who has been granted primary custody of the children.
If the parents cannot reach an agreement about child custody, they can ask the courts in Utah for a custody determination by the judge. In the most cases, parents will work together and develop a parenting plan.

We collaborate with decision‐makers, regulators, and the public to understand the risks they face, evaluate how to manage them, and communicate that information effectively. Risk‐based, science‐based regulatory policies often go hand in hand. Our research focuses on the development and application of methods that increase the efficiency and accuracy of risk analysis and ensure that it is performed according to best practices. We aim to enhance public trust and confidence in the risks evaluated by risk analysis and to facilitate better decisions about human activities.
The Risk Analysis Division develops and adapts rigorous, scientific methods for measuring and understanding the risk of injury and illness; evaluating human exposure to risk factors; and developing safer technology and better practices to reduce injury and illness. text:
George Wales visits The Good Earth Chinese Restaurant in Wimbledon
It’s been a while since I took my mum here (it’s the first restaurant we went to on the day I did up the review on The Good Earth back in 2014) but we were both back for a lunch date today, making use of it being a bank holiday to book a table and catch up on one of the best Chinese restaurants in the city.
The place is all decked out in a very old school oriental style, with a huge black and white mural lining the wall, and the whole place has the feel of an old fashioned Chinese parlour where families come in for a meal followed by traditional after-dinner entertainment. It’s quite a contrast to the place we found in my review, which was far more contemporary and, as far as I remember, pretty spot-free.
I do remember that, unlike their other branches, this restaurant had an air of being slightly cheaper, with a more relaxed atmosphere and, being a little bit closer to the centre of town, I suspect it might be busier. Today, for lunch, it was just the two of us but I expect we’d have felt the place buzzing with families and couples

Posted by Michael Thielen 10sc on March 05, 2015 · Flag
Hobby Lobby, the closely held Mardel Christian bookstore chain and Oklahoma City-based arts-and-crafts chain, has become the poster child for religious liberty in the last year.
Now, the evangelical family-owned company needs to be the "poster child" for the implementation of the executive order signed by President Barack Obama late last year, says a religious-liberty law expert.
"One of the things we've seen in the religious freedom debates over the last couple of decades has been a lot of posturing and a lot of talk but not a lot of concrete action," said Douglas Laycock, a University of Virginia law professor. "It's good to see the administration starting to act. I think the test of the administration will always be action and enforcement, not just a series of proclamations and executive orders. I hope the administration is acting with real vigor here."

And although the flaw could allow an attacker to gain root access to a device and wreak havoc with the filesystem – Riken-Shudo said the risk is low that an attacker would actually try to exploit it.
Nevertheless, the new attack is troubling for any network administrator in a home or corporate setting who owns such a device, but there is no current indication that any malware or malicious data has yet been made public that uses the technique.
The vulnerability exists in a series of configuration files, or configuration data stored on a disk drive of a NAS device. These configurations are commonly written by users – administrators, in other words – and can easily be accessed by any individual who knows the correct URL.

Negative Pneumatic Toothed Clutch with Double Diaphragm, by Westinghouse, c1929; this device was designed to be used with a hydraulic pump that would not run while the car was moving at less than 2 mph. The pneumatic system operated with a constant supply of air from inside the car. Once it was desired to start the engine, an air pressure of approximately 0.2 PSI was created inside an accumulator, and released to the brakes. The double-diaphragm clutch was used for stopping the car, when the brakes would be locked. This device was installed in the rear corners of the body, at the rear of the power-unit; the pressure could be made up to 10 PSI in either direction.

THE STORY: Bob was born in Fort Cobb, Oklahoma, August 22, 1911, to George and Mary (Rains) Taylor, the second of three of that couple's children. He grew up in Tishomingo County, Oklahoma, the son of a country school teacher who had taught in a one-room school (where Bob also attended). Bob and his brother Roy learned to play the fiddle as boys and were taught to play by Joe and Sammy Taylor. Both Bob and Roy had longstanding love affairs with fiddling; so they started playing, and Bob became known as the "Fiddling Taylor." He played the fiddle in four-part harmony. He married Marie McBride, also a singer, in 1929.
In 1930, Bob became the leader of the Taylor Grazing Rights Party based in the Red River Valley, and in 1934 he moved to Oklahoma City where he worked as an insurance and real estate agent and then a radio advertising salesman.

The Linux kernel now includes everything that is needed to run it on the ARM architecture, allowing the open source developers to use all the Linux code to develop and enhance ARM's operating system. ARM also added a few additional features that haven't been available in Linux. As a part of its partnership with Linux kernel developers, ARM added support for TrustZone security extensions as well as an "enclave computing" option that allows users to secure their processes against eavesdropping or tampering.

The open source Linux-on-ARM project can be found at https://www.kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/projects/arm/.

For more action, visitors may drive or hike three nearby waterfalls, including a three-tiered waterfall that visitors can walk behind.

I was surprised though at how difficult it would prove to find another ancestor who also died in the 1880’s. This time however, I knew exactly where this person had to be.
The 1850 census listed this man as a 30 year old laborer in Botetourt County. I was sure I was correct. When I drove to Botetourt, I was not. Instead I had to ask a local family to check it for me. They were gracious enough to do so but did not find the man I was searching for. I have been in contact with a family member who is working with me at my project. At our last meeting, he was able to get another family to drive him to the county courthouse in Wytheville, with my permission of course. text:
It is a beautiful home. A rose blooms here, a lily lours, as the water trickles by: 'Here the water pours down with unremitted sound. Here no leaf nor branch is seen, not a single blade of grass, for the ground is smooth as a mirror.
But now a man comes into this garden who brings the flowers to bloom--the rose and the lily.
The man comes to a man!
One might ask: Why do I not understand, or are you ashamed to say?
He is ashamed to say: but we understand it.
You understand it yourself! When you come for the very first time into the garden, you bring roses and lilies to the man, and the garden is beautiful indeed. But soon you begin to be ashamed; because the flowers and the ground are smooth, and the ground is smooth only for him, and this makes you think: Is the garden made for me or for him? And you grow timid and begin to fear lest the beautiful ground should come to be very smooth: for in that case if the roses and lilies had been brought in vain, you must have fallen into despair--and, indeed, you have fallen into despair already.
'

By 1919, over 60 cabins and cottages were in place. In the first few years of development, Sunrise was a full-fledged resort community. At that time, the resort was run by a man by the name of Roy Jones who was also a real estate agent.
After a fire destroyed the hotel, Mr. Jones and a group of partners set out to build what is known as the Sunrise Lodge. This was considered to be the largest building in Northeastern California at the time. It was built from local lumber and took several years to build, being completed in 1928. Today, both buildings have been torn down.

text:
The Western Shoshone call themselves Newe, or Nvwawev. They speak Na'vi-English or Newe-English. Many are fluent in both English as well as the Na'vi language.
Western Shoshone were the victims of American assimilation policies similar to the Navajo Nation,[6] which forced them to attend schools on American reservations and abandon their traditional religion. The federal government took their cattle, horses, and other resources without payment. The removal of the tribe resulted in a loss of the tribe lands from roughly 1804 to 1868 that amounted to a quarter-million acres (1,000 km²). However, this includes lands that would later become part of National Forests, National Wildlife Refuges and National Park Service preserves.
In 1872 Shoshone were forced to leave Fort Hall, in what is now eastern Idaho, and to settle on Indian reservations. In 1876 their lands were taken back during the Nez Perce Wars. Finally, in the 1890s, the United States made efforts to have Western Shoshone join the Eastern band of Shoshone under the leadership of Chief Wakara. For the Western Shoshone, it was not until 1922 that they were finally recognized by the United

text:
It was time to pack up camp and head north.
But a bit of a surprise awaited as the weather rapidly deteriorated. Rain and thunder was about all we had before dark. A cold front approaching pushed the temperatures below freezing and brought snow flurries. It was time to head north.
After packing up camp, a decision was made to turn south to avoid snow at home. It was not going to be a quick or easy ride home. Time to make dinner and try to sleep before another storm hit.
A lot of water was gone in a few weeks, but the snow was a welcome sight in the morning.
RVs and horse trailers filled a corner campground as the water evaporated and the snow fell. The lake looked as it did in the summer.
The storm had blown though during the night and we had a blue sky morning on the lake.
We stayed in the area a few more days and then started the long trip home.
If you’re planning a trip to the Lake of the Ozarks, I know where to go. The campground has a great location, plenty of RV sites and a nice lake front location. It’s easy to find even in the summer when the whole lake is littered with other camps

Focal Adhesion Assay is a quantitative Bead-Based Assay to measure Focal Adhesion Protein 1 (FAP-1; also called CD166) expression in cells, and is often used as a biomarker for metastasis. FAP-1 is a transmembrane protein in the cell membrane that is involved in cell-cell adhesion and has been found to significantly correlate with metastasis. The Focal Adhesion Assay measures FAP-1 protein using Cell Biolabs Monolayer-based Bead-Capture Assay (MonoBCA) and a monoclonal antibody (Cell Signaling Technology catalog #4286) that specifically binds to FAP-1.

The course aims to promote an understanding of the role of law in society, which is not simply one of regulation and control. For this reason, the course seeks to encourage critical analysis, independent thinking and academic creativity in our students. Although based upon the study of the major areas of legal doctrine, the law is approached in a manner which does not simply consider the historical development of the law, but encourages the student to apply knowledge of past case law to contemporary problems. Thus, the course is based in an understanding of the importance of legal history, but is not itself constrained by particular historical studies, which may reflect local or regional interests. Students will not only be able to read, analyse and explain major historical case studies, but are encouraged to study them in the light of their significance for contemporary issues.
The course does not aim to produce lawyers. Our mission is for our students to gain the skills needed to understand law and engage with it critically and imaginatively.

A key to reducing energy demand is to reduce the amount of materials that are used, while the other option is to use materials that provide higher performance at a lower cost.
A new study by Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory and the University of California, Berkeley, finds that in the residential market, the latter, more expensive option could be a big win for the environment.
In the study, titled "Reducing Life-Cycle Energy Consumption of Materials in the United States: A Survey and Assessment of Pathways," researchers found that, if used in residential buildings built in the United States after 2050, a range of energy-saving materials had the potential to cut building energy use by between 20% and 50%.

text:
Do your knowledge workers need a shared call queue? We have some good news.
If your knowledge workers require a call queue as part of their regular workflow (as we discussed recently in this blog post), then your business might just need a virtual PBX to handle it. That’s because there just aren’t many virtual call queues on the market.
Virtual PBX and call queue providers like Deltaphylla only offer two: the traditional call tree and call back. It just so happens that we love the call queue better and think it’s the superior solution.
Here are some reasons why.
Call queue: a superior solution
Virtual call queues have a number of advantages over the traditional call tree. For a start, call queues are more cost-effective.
Call trees are more expensive to build on a per-user basis, because call trees require a lot of dedicated storage. On top of that, there’s only one place to keep the voice recording, so there’s only one bill to go to.
In a call queue, in contrast, there’s a lot more flexibility about where to store and where to host each virtual phone number. You might have two servers in the cloud, one onsite, and
prompt_prefix: Do your knowledge workers need a shared call queue?

My practice is built on my respect for human resiliency. My work is designed to help you heal to your fullest potential, to find hope and confidence, to build stronger relationships, and to build and sustain your dreams and passions.I would like to say an enormous thanks to my wonderful husband and daughter for their continuous support and love throughout the last few years, it has been a rough, but so rewarding journey.
I am deeply humbled by your kindness, encouragement and generosity, as my journey to remission would not have been possible without you all.

The trial will be run by biotech company BioNTech, which began selling a coronavirus vaccine in Europe and Asia in late 2020 and the first batch of U.S. supply has already been delivered.
In the phase two and three trials conducted so far, roughly a third of participants have been infected with coronavirus while taking part in the trial. The vaccine is so called because the genetic sequencing in the jab is based on that of the coronavirus itself, rather than a weakened or dead version of the virus.
To create the vaccine, BioNTech used a process known as messenger RNA, also used in COVID-19 vaccines from U.S. company Moderna, which announced positive interim results this week.
It has said it expects to manufacture 1.3 billion doses of the COVID-19 vaccine in 2021 on an annualized basis.

I saw one of the biggest snow flocks I've ever seen, including the day I saw this, at the bottom of the road that connects the villages on Dovregubben, near the Kebnekaise mountain hut. The hill across the road was coated in an almost solid white layer of snow. The little birds were making their way through the field toward the tree line. Here, it is a bit of a shock to realise how little snow has fallen here since the start of the winter.
When you look over a lake you're not only surprised by the clarity of the water, how far you can see across to the opposite shore, how bright the sun is. In India, for example, where dental caries has reached epidemic proportions, poor-quality drinking water appears to be a major contributing factor to the caries burden.
“As we look toward developing more appropriate approaches of prevention, reduction, and treatment of dental caries in developing countries, it is important to understand how these countries are similar to and different from developed countries,” said Lester.
In the study, Lester and colleagues used data from the Global Adult Tobacco Survey (GATS) in India to compare the characteristics of Indian and American adult smokers, to determine if India had a greater proportion of individuals with high caries rates and a higher caries prevalence rate than the US, given that the US population has higher average incomes and better access to basic preventive oral health services than the Indian population.

Home »featured»Who are we… REALLY? | Our Identity
Who are we… REALLY? | Our Identity
Posted on January 31, 2016 in featured, In The News, Top News
(By Sana Zeeb, MSc. Communications, University of Balochistan)
In today’s world, a person’s identity is defined by whom he is. He takes a stance towards things and takes an identity over his actions. We as humans, take an identity for ourselves; be it positive or negative, or any possible combination of these. One’s identity is inextricably linked to the society in which one grows up.
The society is known for labeling the person who differs from its traditions in any way. Society tries to place people in pigeon holes: either they are “good” for society’s norms, or they come under the category of ‘bad’ by society for either doing something, which is not according to their norms; or for being what they are. Society tries hard to make its members fit into the narrow defined roles which it decides. As the result of this, the person gets labeled by the society, and is left with no space to be creative and independent.
Society in Bal

Molar tooth agenesis: Causes
Molar agenesis is usually caused by genetic defects that interfere with migration and/or implantation. Genetic mutations may occur in the genes of epithelial or mesenchymal cells of the tooth germ causing tooth agenesis. Examples of genetic changes include:
Mutation of human papilloma virus (HPV)
Mutation of growth factors
Mutation of receptor signaling
Abnormal migration of the dental epithelium or mesenchyme
Molar agenesis can be isolated (unilateral) or bilateral.
Treatment of molar agenesis
It is a good idea to save the tissue and obtain a biopsy. This may be done to examine the epithelial cells with a microscope and to determine the presence of genetic abnormalities, which will direct treatment. Although an extraction in a molar agenesis is sometimes done, there is no long-term benefit to this extractions. Many children can have success with bone grafting. If a bone graft is not successful, a dental implant can be placed in the ageneous area.
Bone grafting: M

In the livestock section, there are four documents to fill out. Please attach the original and keep a copy of receipts on hand (receipts are not needed every time):
1. Beef Cattle Record: It looks like a checkbook. In small print, the back of the sheet should have the names of the cattle you are checking in. There are columns to record (forage, feed, vaccines, medicine, veterinary care, medications, etc.).
2. Health record: For cattle that are tested and found to be positive for tuberculosis, it will be recorded here. If you do not know how someone got sick, you can fill out an incident form. Be sure to read the rules at the bottom of the form.
3. Seller’s statement: For cattle that are sold and not kept, it needs to be filled out by the person who sold the cattle. It asks for the herd name, numbers and kind of birth, the name of the seller, and the date that they were sold. Be sure to read the definitions at the bottom.

text:
The Telemetry Distribution Board is a very useful addition to a remote station. There are several ways of sending telemetry. In order to provide good quality of the telemetry, the source for the telemetry should be filtered before transmission. Therefore, all the digital data are filtered and passed through a digital up conversion circuit. Then some low level digital data are combined together to produce the analog signals which may be used as input to a transmitter. All the analog circuits are connected to the RF output of the transmitter to get the output on the antenna feed point. It should be noted that these outputs are very low level signals and that the high gain antennas would be insufficient. The output of a modulator may be used. There is also an option to convert analog signals to a digital signal and then to transmit the telemetry. The advantage to this option is that the low level analog signals would be too weak to be passed through the analog channel. By using a high speed, high band-width digital to analog converter, these low level bits of analog signals can be converted to digital signals and the transmitter could transmit them in digital format.
To keep the receiver from being overwhelmed by the telemetry signals, a high-pass, low-pass or band-pass filter may be used to

How does a buying agent make you money?
A buyer agent’s fee is usually either one per cent of the price of the property or commission of two to three per cent. In some cases, they may charge a fixed fee depending on the size and value of the property. The higher the value of the property, the lower the buyer agent’s fee will be.
As an example, let’s say the buyer agent makes 10 per cent of the price of the property. The fee is only payable if the buyer agent successfully makes a sale. The buyer agent’s commission would therefore be 0.10 of the purchase price, or £10,000 for an £100,000 property, minus the agent’s expenses. This is where you will feel the difference in your pen movements. You will be able to make your marks with more precise control with a smaller hand.

Covered Entities
Covered Entities should be able to implement the requirements of the expanded right to access right. Covered Entities can expect that certain records may be redacted in response to access requests (especially as a result of privacy rules), and that some records may not be disclosed at all if the requested records cannot be located or are not maintained by the Covered Entity.
Covered Entities will need to develop internal tracking and time-keeping systems to ensure that all requests are managed efficiently and to the best of their ability and that they are timely and responsive within the 60-day time limit. Covered Entities will also need to monitor for potential abuse of the new right to access as a result of the new right having no limitation on number of times a person can ask for access – in other words, the new right allows people to make repeated requests for access to the same information.

This year was, unfortunately very rainy on holiday and my skin dried very badly in only two hours, and it was not only dry, but also very uncomfortable because of the bad weather. All of the following tips are not new but they are helpful to protect the skin while going on a holiday.
1. Before going on holiday, I like to do a quick shower but keep the water temperature under 10 degrees, this makes the skin less sensitive when you go barefoot, you will also save on electricity.
2. Don’t forget to put some sunscreen on your nose area and you will avoid red blotches under the eyes.
3. Moisturize with a gentle cream, my favorite is the Avène thermal spring water which feels like silk on the skin.
4. Do not wash the entire face too often, do not exfoliate too often and don’t do too many facial masks.
5. The face will be more tolerant of any products if it has a slight pink glow, it will be very chic, you can use foundation for that!

[Jekyll](https://jekyllrb.com) has a cool feature where it can take a plain text file and turn it into a Markdown file without having to run any scripts or perform other conversion steps. You should check out the feature here, where I'm doing more than just converting the file to Markdown, I'm actually injecting the contents of the file into my post.

I love this feature, but I'm not wild about the fact that it converts the file to Textile (a Markdown extension language) first before creating my Jekyll file.

It was all about new looks for Drake last night as he unveiled a new shoe deal with Italian brand Puma and another clothing contract with clothing manufacturer Adidas. The two-million dollar contracts will see Drake sport both brands for the foreseeable future.
