If you haven’t already bought tickets, you might be in luck as the rapper is now set to include a second night in a number of venues. After a stop-off in Manchester on November 28, the rapper is set to play at Newcastle Metro Arena just over a day later. The second night at the Arena will also bring a slight change in line-up for fans of Drake. Although it will still be a concert filled with rap, R&B and pop, the artist will forgo his usual hip-hop background in order to bring a more “mainstream” show to the North East.

Kent Anderson – ‘Reveriendødt: Music Made Of Reversed Recordings’ (Faitiche) 4/5
The post-jazz-post-everything-dude in this month’s Music In Notes column, James Wilson-White, once said that "the most amazing thing" about music from Faitiche label "was how so much of it sounded like real music... the music itself is everything, no matter how wild its construction, how unexpected the juxtapositions, how weird the overall effect." He's right.
To take one recent Faitiche-issued record, Jürg Frey's Music For Violin And Piano, the first sounds we hear are the strings of the piano, lightly tapped, and then the notes disintegrate into a thin hiss. Then, a few seconds in, a few notes appear, and then we’re on to the next thing, the next thing, the next thing - "I really don’t know what this music is, or what it is for, but I sure am glad to hear it." It's beautiful, it's beautiful, it's beautiful, it's weird and off-putting and lovely, it's music for people

Our next event “Art History Week: Conventions & Counter Artifacts”. Explore history through a series of lectures and panel discussions examining the art of the past through its relationship to cultural trends, historical developments, and social contexts.
On view will be the work of artist Eric N. Mack, the Department of the History of Art’s 2015-2016 David C. Driskell Visiting Fellow.
Mack’s work investigates the histories embedded in the objects and materials of the African diaspora in the Americas. His installation will feature a new series of collages on paper, three works on Plexiglas, and five sculptures created with antique tapestry fragments and discarded blankets.
The installation incorporates images of textiles from the University of Iowa Museum of Art and historical artifacts from the University of Iowa’s Carl S. Swisher Collection of Ethnography. Also included will be Mack’s work “The Weatherman” (2015), a sculpture inspired by a painting by Charles White depicting the struggle of a child worker in the American South. The work will be featured in the museum’s Swisher gallery, where the artist will give a special tour.
“Conventions & Counter Artifacts” will also include a panel discussion entitled

Gardella was formed initially, at about 1800. The family’s roots are mainly from the Piedmont area, which lies in northwest Italy, in a region called Liguria as well as in Lombardy. In more recent times, the family has moved to central and southern Italy. The name is derived from the Latin ‘gar’, which means man, and the suffix ‘ellis’, usually means small, but here may mean the end man, as in the ending ‘ella’ of ‘alla’. One family member had a large estate in the south of Piedmont, in the commune of Alba, which the family sold at auction in 1875. The family was very much divided with different elements in the south while others were in the north of the country. Many were involved in the wool industry and many had other commercial activities. It is assumed that these included the export of wool to Britain and, at some time, from Ireland. But the Gardellas had come to trade with Ireland in earlier centuries. One of the most famous Nigerian stories is the story of the Ewe people. The Ewe people are believed to be one of the indigenous tribes of Nigeria. However, like many other tribes in Nigeria; the true history of the Ewe people is shrouded in secrecy. What is known about the Ewe people is that they are one of the first people groups whose origin is traced back to the present Niger-delta area.

A former New Mexico military official admitted stealing over five weapons and giving five other rifles to a fellow law enforcement officer, Lt. Col. David H. Patterson pleaded guilty Monday. At sentencing Nov. 21, Patterson will be ordered to pay a $10,000 fine, said a New Mexico State Police spokesman, Sgt. Elizabeth Armendariz.
Patterson, a retired police officer with the State Police, gave two Colt 45 caliber guns – including a loaded gun – to fellow New Mexico State Police employee Col. Javier Peña on January 21, 2013, Armendariz said. Peña pleaded guilty to one criminal count of tampering with evidence on August 10, 2012, she said.
Peña is awaiting sentencing Nov. 21; his trial was recently postponed until Aug. 5, 2014, she said.
Peña gave the firearms to his son-in-law, Brian Vangold, an employee with the State Police. Authorities recovered the guns from Vangold, she said. Both charges are second-degree felonies, she said.
In exchange for these weapons, Peña allegedly received over $1,500 from Vangold, who pled guilty to one charge of tampering with evidence in the summer of last year

You got stuck working on a story after being unable read it because everything went well, until there was. Now you have a very important story, to the point of it being an urgent issue of national security, but nothing could be done. The entire nation relied on you to save it from extinction, but you now can't see and read anything. You look around, and see a bunch of people wearing lab coats rushing in different directions, saying weird things like "the formula is unstable". The words and pictures are all moving and changing around the screen, and you can't tell what's going on. You have an important message, about something happening in the world or at your high school, but you can't understand anything other than what's on the screen. The voices and words don't make sense. You look behind you, and see the man who told you to work on that story, but he's different. The news anchor that was looking straight into the camera is now frowning. He looks angry, and his words don't work with your ears. The words keep jumping and changing around him. This is how you wake up.
The most powerful word processor in the world would be able to help us get it's name, "the news printer".

What Do Cabbage Bud Breakdown Mean? (Truth inside the Story behind it!)
Cabbage bud breakdown is an indication that the plant is dying. The cabbage has no hope and the plant will be killed by lack of water. Cabbage bud breakdown can also be an indication of plant stress and the plant is losing water through the leaves or the petioles.
When the plant shows signs of cabbage bud breakdown, it’s advisable to cut off all growing heads. Remove the plant entirely with all the remaining foliage and roots before applying any foliar spray such as Iron and Azaline. Don’t waste your money on this foliar spray as it will do nothing to help the plant.
If the plant is not showing any sign of growth, then you can treat it with nitrogen and foliar spray, but it won’t matter anyway because it’s already dead. Don’t use any compost fertilizer because it will attract pests and diseases.
See also: Fertilizer for Cabbage (Complete Guide)

The author of Misogamya achyrosora in the second edition of his monograph on the genus Pennisetum (Muma 1955), which was published by the Imperial Agricultural College of Japan, writes:
"Misogamya is a small species related to Pennisetum villosum, but differs in the character that the leaves are densely hairy along the veins, the inflorescence is a spike composed of numerous capitula, and the fruits in the form of dry heads."
The author's description is not satisfactory for the names of these species, and the term "Misogamya" appears to be an improper name.
Muma also mentions, for example, the plants Misogamya sericea or "Misogamya sericeata" in two places, for example in the text and in a list. Misogamya sericea Muma was described by C. C. Berg in 1912 and belongs to the synonym list of the present species.

This article focuses only of online learning environments within Open Learn (OL), Open Science Europe's (OSE) e-learning platform.
Open Learn, launched in late 2003 as a resource on open educational resources (OER), has since grown to become a central OSE portal. It provides a range of information on open science, including case studies from across Europe detailing how to run and implement open science projects, and a library of educational resources. It also hosts a number of OSE projects, including the Open Knowledge Foundation's Open Data Institute.
Open Learn began as a resource site. In June 2003, then OSE Manager, Richard Poynder, decided that OL needed to be rebranded and rebuilt. The OL homepage featured a rotating banner containing information on OSE, a list of projects with links to each, and a news page with updates on both OSE's and Open Learn's progress - this page is still available in the 'about' section (although it is no longer updated).

This page documents details provided and updated over HTTP endpoints for the Cloud Big table index maintenance feature. The endpoint format includes GET requests that return data for the current cluster, but also POST requests that require users to provide data for each table they want to add indexes to.
The following image depicts data for a cluster that has been provisioned, contains some tables, and that has already been configured for maintenance operations. The following image depicts an index configuration table created in the UI that has been submitted via an API endpoint for processing by a maintenance script.
The cluster ID parameter specifies a unique identifier for a single cluster. This parameter is the only required part of the URL. The cluster ID can be obtained from the Dashboard, or for any cluster provisioned for maintenance purposes.
The tables URL parameter contains a table list, which refers to one or more tables. The table IDs specified within this parameter must be unique, or else an error will be returned when executing a GET operation.
When the GET API endpoint receives a table list that contains more than one table ID, or multiple table list parameters, the resulting API call returns with all the tables listed.

This is a comprehensive data collection from NASA that is a result of the Earth System Data Record (ESDR) Program designed to improve our ability to monitor the planet's global climate, its changes, and their effects on the environment and society to aid global responses to environmental issues and natural hazards.
ESDR provides a global set of time series data products that span a range of science applications. The data types include: geophysical variables (clouds, aerosols, fire, water components, and solar radiation); land surface temperature and surface precipitation; sea surface temperature and sea surface height; and ocean color.
ESDR data are made available through NASA's Global Climate Change Data Center (GCDC).
Global Fire Emissions Database (GFED) is a set of gridded daily fire emissions (smoke, CO2, etc) from the North American, South American, Asian, and African regions

What exactly made Kratie (which I pronounced "Caratty," in my best Australian brogue) the most popular stop? It's the closest to Cambodia's border with Vietnam, and the most likely to see Mekong River dolphins that swim across the river every day. It's also the location of the best-known temple and the only stupa in the country -- not to mention a nice little town with some good food, cheap, and easy to find accommodation and the best street-side food stalls. Not to mention its proximity to the other most interesting city I'd visited so far -- the border town of Phimai.
The dolphinarium is not my thing, but I felt for these poor dolphins, put on display. The guide said that in another country, these "show" dolphins would be slaughtered. There is a small sanctuary for rescued dolphins in the Philippines, and I wondered if any of their offspring lived there.

It’s amazing that so much can be crammed into so small and simple a form, but when everything is working in lockstep, it creates one very complex and layered piece of art.
And, of course, you can’t listen to an entire movement of this symphony all in one sitting, and I’ve heard the music so, so many times that I don’t remember the details any more. It does have some of my all-time favorites: the opening section “Lacrymosa” (which is just an amazingly beautiful melody), then “Requiem Aeternam” (which is equally beautiful, sad, then turns into an absolutely rocking section, then comes back up to the opening melody)…but I love all of it, all the way to the very simple but effective way it ends.
I have my favorite recordings of this piece, but they’re all good. It

Cherubim Press of Santa Clar, from Michael Jansen via Steve Finley... “The publisher of the Cherubim Press in Santa Clara, California was a man named A.J. Wiebalck. He was born in Germany in 1825 and emigrated to the U.S. as a young man. Shortly after settling in San Francisco he got a job working at the press of his former employer, Christian Wenzel, an esteemed publisher of the day. He fell in love with one of the Wenzel girls and married them both. At some point he was asked by Wenzel to go to Santa Clara for a while to start a new press for that small community. Shortly afterward he was asked to move there full-time. So he did and established a small press there, one of many at the time.
In the late 1800s there were nearly 15 presses in the Santa Clara valley, some of which were producing as many as 60,000 books per year. A.J. started his press in his own home until he moved into the town hall, which at the time had been converted into his print shop. I did go thru both this year’; they could’ nt go by the post as they were so big!
Wanted to take more photos but it was so hot and I didn’t have any iced drinks, so I got back to my room and chilled!
Then off to the next village for a picnic by the river. Once again all very organised by Gareth and his helpers. I sat by the river and ate and watched the fish!
Then to a little old building which was the first bank in Wales to be built in 1809! So it would be worth an hour or so of reading the information that was there about it’s history!
Then on the 2.30pm train and back to Cardiff, with an added extra stop at Carmarthen for a meal, where there was a great atmosphere, with all the coaches being filled with Welsh people celebrating the success of their team!
So another fun, successful trip, and a great way to celebrate and show our true Welsh patriotism to the world!!

By Ksenia Sukhanova
Mogens Hvild Nielsen, one of the creators of the Danish folk-rock music of 1960s and ’70s, the artist who also became the first person in the world to undergo IVF treatment, passed away on September 28.
The cause of death was sudden heart failure caused by old age. He was ninety, and his wife Eva was by his side. Nielsen died at home in Copenhagen, according to his daughter Marie.

Sitting alongside Kobe City Airport with its Kita Railway on his left was another Kobe airport, also called Kobe, also in Kobe City, Kobe Prefecture, Japan. If you squint, you can make out the outline of its passenger terminals.
I thought it was interesting that there were two airports in the city limits of Kobe.
Kobe Airport (神戸空港, Kōbe Kūkō) or Kobe Airport, was a municipal airport in Kobe, Japan, located on an artificial island in a bay of the Seto Inland Sea about 1 km west (but closer to the city than the sea) from the mouth of the Yodo River.
Today, the Kobe Airport site is home to the Ōsaka University Kobe City Campus and the Osaka University School of Dentistry Kobe Dental Center.
In the early 1990s, the name of Kobe Airport was changed to Kansai International Airport. While it was still under construction, it was popularly referred to as “Nanko Skyport (南子空港, Nanko Kūkō)” or "Nankō", which was eventually dropped by the media

Piano students study an effective lesson with teacher David Pichier for his music lesson in Pianos Plus Music studio in Sydney. We look at the keyboard with David and discover that he is not just teaching piano, but he is teaching skills and techniques for reading music. Learning to play the piano is about getting the basics right, and there are many piano lessons in Sydney that are teaching students the fundamentals and other essentials required to play the piano well.
Music is a great way to study, it can help you succeed. In music, it is all about practice. When a student is learning piano, they need to practice and listen to their teacher. David Pichier recommends that students begin with the music that they know. Learning to read at the piano is a good way to continue your success with students, and music is one of the most valuable tools for learning in the classroom.
Music is important to learn about, in Australia and throughout the world. There are music teachers everywhere. Music education is a good way to build confidence. Piano lessons in Sydney are an excellent tool for self esteem.

Anthem’ s annual national leadership summit for top performing and well funded grant organizations, the Leading Edge Conference and Summit, took place at the JW Marriott Austin, Texas on February 24-28, 2019. The conference was attended by over 1,300 leaders from more than 1,200 grant organizations across the nonprofit sector. The conference’s primary theme, “The New Era of Social Change: The Future of Nonprofit Leadership,” explored how the leaders of the nonprofit sector need to meet the challenges of the “Fourth Industrial Revolution,” how they can prepare for the future, and how they can ensure that their organizations are future proof.
In order to help nonprofits address the unique challenges of the 21st century, ANTHEM offers leadership training, consulting and assessment services, and other resources to help nonprofits address the unique challenges of the 21st century.
During the past year, nearly 1 million people in the United States received the lifesaving medical care and health resources they need, thanks to grants from the American Heart Association. These grants serve to help communities nationwide address the critical health needs of the communities, with a total worth of $40.6 million awarded to nearly 500 programs across the United States.

Eating, Spelling - New School
Kirk Calvey - Feb 17 @ 15:28pm
The University of Houston will offer an advanced spelling-bee program for fourth, fifth and sixth graders who excel in the school spelling bee.
Called the UH School Spelling Bee Championship, the program is being offered through the UH Department of Teaching and Learning from 10 a.m. to 3 p.m. March 31 in the University Center. It’s open to students across the Houston area who have won their school spelling bee.
The program is designed to give each competitor additional practice with more challenging words and vocabulary. The students will be coached by UH faculty and staff, along with a group of volunteer coaches and a nationally known speaker, according to the News Center. The speakers are from TESOL International Association, an affiliate of the Department of Teaching and Learning.
“We know the words a contestant can spell from school bees are not necessarily words in the same level of difficulty from regional or state competitions,” said David Zitarelli, professor and department chair of Teaching and Learning. “So, this is a place for them to further hone their word knowledge in a fun environment.”

Kudumu-Obiama Jukajika Forest Complex – Sapo Catchment and Community Forest Initiative
The Kudumu-Obiama Jukajika Forest Complex lies between the slopes of Mount Bamburu to the South, Mount Ggabatut and Mount Mbangwadza to the East, and the shores of Lake Malawi to the west. The landscape of the community forest area consists of grassland ridges interspersed with small patches of secondary forest, which can be seen as strips of vegetation along the streams, hills or river banks. Bamboo, figs, thorn trees and some palms can be seen along the ridges.
The Kudumu-Obiama Jukajika Forest Complex is located in Nkhata bay, Mangochi District, southern Malawi. The boundaries of the protected areas are defined by streams that are sources of permanent streams and rivers or are wetlands which provide water and fish species used for consumption, local medicinal use, and commercial fishery at community fishponds. A small (7 inches; 14 in cap circumference; 0.4 in beard thickness), round, dry (5 - 8 months), red pepper-tipped Coratina is a medium-hot to hot pepper. One of my favorite peppers as it has a wonderful, earthy flavor that doesn't give you a bad taste in your mouth. I like to use it to make hot sauces and salsas as its a great all around pepper. We grow ours in a greenhouse and it is very drought tolerant. Our greenhouse is only heated if the weather is really cold and not usually until the middle of January. In the spring we use a drip-irrigation system that helps keeps the soil moist but allows the roots to get plenty of water. Our peppers get a lot of sunshine (especially in summer). One of the things about peppers is that if they are planted in the garden and you don't give them good drainage and shade, they will produce a bad fruit. They need lots of sun but need some shade when they are in heat.

CULINARY USES:
Peppers are high in Vitamin A, Potassium, Folate and Manganese

Antaramera River (or Mavazo Ilanguana on Shangaan; /'antaraməə(ɹ)/ is a river in the east of Zimbabwe. It flows from its sources south of Chimanimani Mts through the eastern portion of Chimanimani Park to the Zambezi River. It has long been proposed as a possible source for the Zambezi River due to the similarity of size to the Zambezi.

The main source for the river is at Mavazi Hills. A stream flowing down the hill known as Mtoto Moto ("child's play") is then called the Ngutwa River. The name Antaramera River comes from the Tsonga's Ngutwa Ngutwa (child's play) to Ngut'wa (the stream or river). It is sometimes referred to as the Chikundura River, as it does not run through the Chimanimani Mts.

Waste from medical care should be stored to minimize damage during handling before use.
The medical waste is treated in terms of safety before handling by using a sterilization process such as steam.
Therefore, the sterilized object is stored in a stainless steel box to ensure safety during transportation.
In addition, the medical waste is packaged in a bag called “bag for disposal or reuse”.
The bag for disposal or reuse is used for transporting the medical waste.
It is designed to prevent leakage and damage while maintaining the content of the medical waste from the leakage.
The bag for disposal or reuse has a leakproof design such as zippers and is used for both transportation of medical waste to incineration facilities and hospitals, and transfer to the incineration plant.
Waste from medical care is transferred to the incineration plant using a bag for disposal or reuse.
Transfer to the incineration plant, which handles medical waste safely, will not cause a waste of material in accordance with the specifications.
Also, a medical waste incinerator is operated using a sealed system for preventing leakage and maintaining the contents, and then incinerated to eliminate infectious substances.

The New World of Manufacturing and Retail
“Suppliers are becoming more aggressive when it comes to pricing, so retailers are forced to match them to keep up. This is where our focus is.” - Jim Miller, Hoult’s
The new world of manufacturing and retail is a game of cat and mouse. But instead of battling over market share, it’s the race between manufacturers and retailers to gain an edge in pricing power. The result, in both cases, is a focus on cost reduction and a hunt for new ways to increase margin.
Manufacturing’s biggest challenges fall into two categories: improving productivity and increasing flexibility. A few ways vendors are responding: adding capacity by opening green factories and plants; investing in robotics; and implementing lean manufacturing and continuous improvement methodologies. A typical manufacturer spends 5 percent of revenue on marketing, but most of it goes toward just 1 percent of the products. The thinking is that, if they could trim back that marketing spend and instead use it for other, higher-value activities – such as innovation – they’d likely see a big increase in value.

Lithochauxian is a plant taxoclimatoloclogical rank similar to benthic or soil, with a specific climate created by the interactions of geology, climate, and flora.
There are two types of lithochauxiae: lithochauxia bathyale and lithochauxia pelagicae. They are also known as chemotypes and benthic chemotypes. A lithochauxian biome occurs when there is a lithochauxian species growing.
Lithochauxias occur in oceans and seas and on land areas with no soil. The lithochauxiae are often found in benthic or pelagic environments at depths that are typically less than 100 m or below the photic zone of the ocean.
Examples of lithochauxia bathyale biomes include the sea floor of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge and in the Atlantic and Pacific oceans.
Examples of lithochauxia pelagicae biomes include both shallow and deep oceans and seas. The biome type may vary due to the lithochauxial species that is present. The lithochauxia chemotypes most common in the pelagic zone of seas have a typical depth of 10 km and a water density of a

Actress Diahann Carroll dead at 84
1:19 PMDiahann Carroll, who became the first African-American to star in a non-servant role in a TV series, died on Friday night at her home in Los Angeles. She was 84 years old.
A star in her own right, Carroll famously starred in the groundbreaking sitcom “Julia,” about a single working-class black woman who moved out of the South and up the economic ladder. Though the series aired for just one season on NBC starting in 1968 — a period now remembered by critics as the “golden age” of sitcoms — the show was a beacon of hope for people of color seeking television content that reflected them and their experiences.
Before the series ended, in 1971, Carroll had received nine Emmy nominations as best lead actress.
Carroll was also nominated for a Tony Award for her performance in the 1978 musical “No Strings.”

Mies’s skyscrapers are becoming obsolete. But let’s not throw the baby out with the bathwater
The Miesian skyscraper is slowly disappearing from downtown Chicago. Most of the iconic buildings by the great modern architect have found new lives, sometimes with new facades, or have been demolished for parking lots, but the Miesian tower itself appears to be falling from grace. There’s a reason for this, and it isn’t the buildings.
A couple of years ago, the Chicago Tribune published an article explaining why their modernist, glassy skyscraper built in 2004 was losing some $20 million a year on leasing. It’s a bad time to build a tower, in other words. The article was entitled “It’s the Glass, stupid.” In the same piece the author noted the “new, more open-plan concept” that Mies wanted, but also warned that “In the future, these buildings will increasingly be built with fewer windows—something a little less Miesian —because it’s cheaper.”

Posted on Wednesday 14 November 2018 by Paul Gosling
The growing popularity of online payday loans has been a factor behind more than 20 per cent of consumers seeking debt advice, according to analysis by the Money Advice Service.
The findings are based on advice taken from a representative sample of the UK population. The research also revealed that:
the average amount consumers are borrowing has risen from £320 to £360 over the past year (12 per cent increase);
a quarter (26 per cent) of consumers have borrowed £500 or more in the past year; and
the average consumer who accessed payday loans to assist with overdraft or credit card debts had taken out an average of 1.6 loans, worth an average of £1,030.
The study also found that consumers are increasingly taking a ‘break on arrival’ approach, in that they have taken a break before borrowing more. New Study Identes Bipolar Brain Disorders Similar to Alcohol Abusing Patients! The Journal "Neurology"
[A] brain scan study has found that people who are prone to mental health episodes and binge drinking have a very similar neurological footprint.
Researchers examined how the brains of people who had at least one episode of depression in the year leading up to getting scanned.
One of the most widely-used screening tests for depression is the Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression, which assesses the severity of patients' symptoms.
Depression is often accompanied by sleep problems, which in turn leads to excessive daytime sleep.
It was the patients who were most often diagnosed with alcohol-use disorder had the most pronounced hyperconnectivity in the prefrontal cortex, a region of the brain that helps the person to stop and think before they act.
The research, based on brain scans of people with a history of both depressive episodes and binge drinking, found that when depressed individuals abstain from alcohol for one year, they see improvements to their depression and their alcoholism.
The new research found that alcohol users that are trying to quit also see improvements, as long as they abstain from alcohol for one year.

Growth hormone increases protein synthesis only temporarily: Newer findings call established theories regarding stimulation of anabolic processes in muscle cells into question. Growth hormone (GH) has long been known to stimulate protein synthesis in skeletal muscle and to activate the gene for creatine kinase, which provides energy to muscle cells. But new findings from an international research group, which includes scientists from the German Center for Neurodegenerative Diseases (DZNE) in Bonn show that while growth hormone increases the overall activity of protein synthesis in muscle tissue in the short term, it does not cause a lasting increase in muscle mass or strength for more than 5 days. Instead the protein anabolic processes are only activated and remain so for a short period, as if "switched off" after this time. The researchers say their findings could improve treatment for muscle wasting diseases such as cancer, AIDS and muscular dystrophy.
The growth hormone is among the many so-called hormones, specialized signaling molecules that instruct cells to do certain things. By stimulating protein synthesis it has for years been used to counteract muscle loss, for example after illness, surgery or cancer treatments such as chemotherapy. It can also boost the development of muscles during athletic training such as weight training. However, scientists have also observed over the past

Our most recent case involved working under tight financial requirements because two parties decided NOT to communicate, and therefore make plans with their attorneys and experts. Ultimately, the parties settled by making sure that they got what they wanted, and saving more than they would have if they proceeded with trial — and still saving money.
A good mediator can make the difference between settling or deciding to litigate. Here are three tips that help us better guide the parties through negotiations:
1. Know the Parties You are Negotiating with —
What works with one party might backfire with another. There are no guarantees, of course, and we are not psychic, but one of the first things we look at is where the parties agree and disagree, and where their “hot buttons” are. If there are areas of agreement, this can be a good starting point. If there are areas that could make or break the agreement, we can look at ways to narrow those differences. It’s important not to jump to conclusions. Sometimes it is better to let the parties work on their own, and then re-engage them — this leads to a more complete understanding.
2. Don’t Rush Through a Mediation —

Fishing has traditionally contributed towards rural employment in most European coastal MemberStates; yet a closer examination of the industry's value-generation and the factors affectingits production reveals that there exists no homogenous fishing community across the whole ofEurope. This book investigates to what extent, how and why the European Union has become a major source of financial support for various regional and coastal fishing activities in the European Union. In so doing, it provides a critical assessment of the effects of EU support on the sustainability of resource productivity, labour and economic activity in coastal communities and regions. The book includes chapters on the economic rationale for support, the design and effectiveness of various EU financing mechanisms, the interaction between regional and coastal policy and the sustainability of marine resource and coastal communities. In addition, the author discusses the prospects for further support for regional fishing activities under the Common Fisheries Policy Review in the future.

Osteotomy surgery in Delhi may include an ankle or spinal adjustment known orthopedists as a fusion or osteotomy. Osteotomies are surgeries that cut into the bone to increase its stability and allow room for other bone to move into. If needed, the doctor may fuse the bone into place to allow for pain relief and increased functionality of the spinal cord. The surgeon typically cuts the bone from two separate bones to join them and thus prevent fractures.
Osteotomy surgeries are done for several purposes in the spinal field but are also done for ankle purposes as well. Spinal osteotomies may be necessary if two (2) or more vertebrae are being fused, if they are crushed or broken, and if the spine has become unstable in its posture.
Ankle osteotomies are done if a person has arthritis or bone spurs that affect the joint of the foot that are making it hard for them to stand or walk. If the spine is involved in the surgery, it is in conjunction with a fusion in the spinal cord to prevent future fractures to the spine.
Osteotomy surgery is done under general anesthesia which means that the patient is completely asleep during the surgery. The patient will lie on a table that helps to keep them as still and

Megan was educated at Lakeville High School and Minot State University. She worked at Allina Hospitals

I like watching the weather for any signs a good wind is due on our trips in general, and for the past couple of years I’ve noticed a trend of “cool fronts” coming up in the mid-week to Sunday for the start of a trip. These are not as warm as a regular front, but they’re usually stronger than a dry run. And that’s exactly what happened last weekend.
The front began on Thursday of last week and the next morning I was able to go sailing for only a short time before I was chased upwind again. I had to wait till Saturday, when the conditions were just right, the wind was stronger and the sky was blue. Sunday is a good time to sail on windy days because they tend to be less gusty, allowing for more consistent conditions.
Saturday morning was just what I wanted. Stronger winds meant less chance of wind-surfing and the sun would shine on us all afternoon, so I set off with only my wind-surfing sail pack, making the trip more “sporty”.
I decided to sail in the bay and try out an unusual spot, the mouth of The Swilly near Portrush. The river mouth is a favourite for the more experienced

Via Facebook recently shared several ways on its social wall to share files on Facebook and it's actually simpler than you'd think.
With the recent update to Facebook Timeline that makes sharing to Facebook easier, there are several ways to share files in your cover photo or profile picture.
Uploading a picture to the cover photo is very easy, simply drag pictures from your image directory on your computer or mobile device and drop into the cover photo pane.
Uploading images to the profile photo is also rather straight forward and takes only a few minutes to accomplish. Simply use the "Upload Picture" tool from the right hand side and upload the image to Facebook.
A great alternative to the "Upload Picture" tool is the Facebook mobile tool. To access this tool simply enter Facebook from your phone and open the home screen and then "Graph Search". Graph Search was created to find friends or people that may have mutual friends. Once that's finished the "Images" feature will open. Once in Facebook will ask you to "Log In" and then choose "Your Photos" from the left hand side. You can then upload pictures, edit and delete images and then share photos via Facebook.
Lastly, images can be shared with the "Upload Photo" tool within Facebook

Former St Johns College principal dies whilst surfing in Australia after suspected heart attack
November 12, 2019 By admin No comments yet
By Nick McKenzie and Eryk Bagshaw
A retired principal of St John’s College and former deputy chief magistrate of Port Phillip Bay, Edward Poulsons, has died while surfing on the Great Ocean Road in Victoria.
Mr Poulsons, 84, was a former principal of St John’s College in Lonsdale Street Melbourne and former head of the National Judicial College and Australian Academy for Leadership in the Law. He retired as deputy chief magistrate of Port Phillip Bay in June this year.
Port Phillip Magistrates Court chief magistrate Christine Clements described him as an ‘absolute gent’ whom ‘all court users had a great deal of respect and affection for’.
‘His contribution through mentorship of so many judicial and law students was outstanding and greatly appreciated,’ she said. ‘Ed was one of those people who you just loved working with.’

On 2nd September 2015, the BBC released this picture of the lifeless body of Aylan Kurdi. Aylan’s body washed up on Turkey’s coast, three days after he drowned with his family attempting a crossing into Europe. This photo – one of the most tragic and heartbreaking images of our time – has brought about this tragedy once again for a nation of displaced people that is seeking a new home.
Aylan was a Kurdish Syrian refugee. The family and its neighbours fled from A’zaz, a northern Syrian town that was under siege by the war. The Kurdish Democratic Union Party (PYD) were the de facto military rulers in this area which is now under the control of Bashar al-Assad and his ally Russia. In March this year, the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) estimated there were more than 935,000 displaced Syrian Kurds in Turkey, the vast majority of these from A'zaz.

Bellezza (or Lupellata for an easier digestion): One more beauty ingredient to take with you!
Bellezza, or Lupellata Fruits, originate from Mexico. The berries grow on a bush or a vine and have a sweet flavor.
The flowers attract butterflies, beetles, and hummingbirds. It is also a beautiful vine that twines around other trees and shrubs.
They look like small round berries, are 1.5cm in diameter, and have a mild, sweet flavor.
In general, plants with berries are rich in antioxidants and vitamins.
The berries contain a sweet-tasting substance called rhamnetin, a flavonoid (a type of polyphenol).
They also contain other flavonoids such as quercetin, myricetin, and kaempferol. The berries also contain vitamin C, B-vitamins, polyphenols, and other polyphenols and other nutrients.
The berries are a rich source of antioxidants and other nutrients.
For those who are struggling with thyroid function, a diet which consists of a balanced amount of flavonoid-rich foods such as fruits and vegetables is vital.

Dwaine Rutherfor joins his family ranch with enthusiasm & desire—his uncle Billy tells him at the start: “Dwaine, you’re the third generation to take out my mortgage.” Dwaine soon discovers a rich history of hardship, hard work, and a legacy of love that has carried through generations.
An unexpected love story, it also reveals the strength, resilience, and dignity of the men and women who have managed to hold the ranch through hard times—including the Dust Bowl, the Great Depression, and the droughts of the late 1930s and ’40s. In a time when so many have lost hope, this moving narrative reminds us that the human spirit will not be denied, offering inspiration, hope, and the possibility of a better life.
Includes an Epilogue describing how Dwaine Rutherfor, Sr. and, his wife, Gloria, saved and restored the ranch in 1986.

In the event that the benefit is payable as a result of one of the four reasons for benefits indicated in the Social Insurance Act, the benefit amount shall be determined annually and the level of the benefit shall be determined every two years in accordance with the Act. These are a pension, benefits related to a job lost or to an employment ending due to illness, maternity/paternity leave, a disability pension and a survivor pension.
2. In accordance with the Act, the Social Insurance Board pays the benefit, except for an age pension, to an insured person whose claim is approved, or an insured person who is granted a disability pension or a survivor pension whose claim is approved or another category of persons. In accordance with the Act, the Social Insurance Board also pays an age pension to a person who has reached the age of 75 and has been entitled to the same, i.e. has been issued an ID number, for at least 20 years in the calendar year in question. In any case, the Social Insurance Board pays an age pension to a person if he or she no longer works

Dr. Evelyn Nakano Glenn, Professor of U.S. History, UC Berkeley

She began by highlighting the role of Black feminist organizers, such as Mary Church Terrell and Ida B. Wells, and their efforts that laid the foundation for civil rights activism.
Glenn noted the “political activism of Black women that was grounded in an analysis of the structural roots of racial subordination.” She focused her panel presentation on the “double bind” Black mothers faced in attempting to gain access to jobs that allowed them to provide for their families. In this book, we give a rigorous description of a new kind of mechanism that has recently found new application in economics, and that has the same kind of importance as a new toolkit has for structural engineers. In its new form, this method has become a way to model complex situations without having to resort to unrealistic assumptions.
One of the basic features of the current neoclassical economic model is that it deals with a unique equilibrium. As a result, it becomes possible to determine in a quite simple way the characteristics of an equilibrium of the system under investigation, without having to make any particular assumptions about the initial conditions of the model or any restrictions on the range of functions used. The same holds true for mathematical programming and optimization problems.

How Does Eliminates Rally's Dependence From Tires, And Improve Overall Performance?
Eliminates Rally's dependence on tires, and improves overall performance.
It is a simple answer but also a complex one. Eliminates Rally's dependence on tires, and improves overall performance. In other words, for many years, most rally cars have been forced to rely on a tire as the only way to measure grip level. The only option available to measure the grip level between the tires was the grip level on the tire.
The idea behind Rally Ramp is simple. We are leveraging the force in contact with the tire and instead measuring this force through the entire width and length of the tire. This results in a more uniform data point and allows you to measure this force from the tire, which is a much larger area than the tire itself. By creating a point of force reading, we are then able to compare it to a baseline of force to determine the difference in the way that the two cars are driven.
What we find once we have all of these data points from the tire, is that the force generated does not appear linear. In fact, it is actually not linear at all. With a good baseline force reading,

If they have never received a ballot before or don't normally mail back absenties due to financial constraints, they could be forgiven for failing to vote in the upcoming council elections in May.
The EDA is aware of a number of people who have been unable to vote in the last two elections due to a problem.
A further five electors were disenfranchised in 2016 owing to the failure to receive voting forms.
We need to get them all back, says the EDA.
Cllr Richard Wates, chair of the EDA, is calling for the return of absentee postal ballots to be increased from a fortnight, to the previous week.
“What a fantastic opportunity we have here to vote, and no-one should miss out, ” he said.
“It is incredibly important that every vote is counted.
“As it is an election where votes are cast on the same day, there is no way the count can be completed in such a short space of time.
“We are asking voters to do the right thing and return their votes in the same week as voting day - in the same way we do for postal votes.
“This will give more people an opportunity to have their say."

It is the world’s largest ship recycling yard. Gadani ship breaking yards was commissioned by Pakistan in 1969 and presently comprises of 5 ship breaking yards. Gadani is a small industrial area. It was established as a port of call for cargo ships coming from the Middle East to carry out cargo from South Asian and African Continents.
Gadani Ship breaking yards was set up with the help of Pakistan Navy. It was initially setup to dismantle the ships of Pakistan Navy which it had received during the 1971 Indo-Pak War. There are about 1,700 people working in Gadani ship breaking yards. About 25,000 people work in this industrial area of Gadani.
A few countries in Asia and Africa use Gadani ship breaking yards for dismantling the old ships. There was a time when Gadani was recognized as the largest ship recycling site in the world. But since then not many ships have been going to ship breaking yard of Gadani (for its economical condition and environmental condition too). In the last decade the activity has decreased.

I had an absolute privilege last fall participating int an inaugural group in NIAID/FIC’s Innovative Analytical Center for Translational Medicine (ICTM) program. NIAID is part of the National Institutes of Health; ICTM is a collaborative group with FIC that brings together a variety of scientists to perform preclinical studies aimed at translation to the clinic. The goal of the group is to understand the mechanisms underlying autoimmunity and the development of therapies to inhibit the aberrant immune response in diseases such as Sjogren’s syndrome. For the first part of the session we met over the internet – we used Adobe Connect and it worked beautifully. Next, I flew to Baltimore to work in the lab.
For those of you who do not know, the lab I am in is doing translational research on Sjogrens, using two mouse models of the disease. We were able to take advantage of the program to try to understand better, in an immunologic and molecular level, one of the major hurdles in Sjogren's, the development and maintenance of the antibody response on the kidney.

On September 27 the Senate overwhelmingly sent approval message its resolution (Araujo Vega 2/13/2018/SJ), which states that the Government of the Dominican Republic is to be excluded from the Generalized System of Preferences due to its systematic and ongoing failure to cooperate, protect human rights, and fulfill its international commitments. In other words, the Dominican Republic is being excluded not for some isolated incident, but because it is a habitual defiant.
The resolution comes after years of failed attempts to achieve any type of results from the regime of President Danilo Medina. Despite being the second highest contributor to the GDP of the Dominican Republic, the labor conditions are the worst in the hemisphere for agricultural workers. It has led the world’s highest number of murders per capita. Women are the most brutalized group in the country. Human rights defenders are targeted on a daily basis with impunity.
Dominicans for Rep. Dom. Inst.

Italy have taken their bow! Today will be my 6 month milestone to completing BOTH of my first two year's of university in just under 4.5 months!
The biggest lesson I have learned from studying Abnormal Psychology is that in some ways we can be our own worst enemy. We have the capacity to do so much of what we want to, it just comes down to how well we can regulate in the face of a very tough situation. We have the ability that we need to be able to reach our goals in both our academic and professional lives.
When this is the case and we face difficulties, it’s a good idea to look back at where we started and see how far we’ve come along this journey. From my previous posts, you can see that I was not very good a regulating properly at the start of this year. Through learning from my mistakes and the help of many, I am back on track, and I am so much wiser because of it! If you’re facing similar circumstances or if you just feel like things are not really going well, remember this. This may sound rather depressing, but I assure you that it’s not, I’m just trying to motivate myself and other people around me.

Dhammasigka Theera Kandhuwe Maityadhattiye Mahatheriye Dhammasiggiyasu was born to a rich family of a business tycoon of Jaffna in Sri Lanka and was the younger brother of the famous politician and poet Thiruthanikamal Maityadas. Mahavihare, as he is affectionately known in the Buddhist circles, was born in the Vihariyamunai neighborhood of the city, in the year of 1843.
Though he could not attend the schools as he was the only one in the family to stay in the home, as his father was in the field of trade. Thus, he was largely an autodidact, he was also a brilliant student. At the age of 17, he joined the Jaffna Maha Vihara as a pupil of his great grandfather. As a young boy, he mastered both the Pali and Sanskrit. He also mastered the Abhidhamma. Having studied the Dravyasiddhi and Viyutthu, it is reported that at the age of 21, he went to the Ananda Samavasana, the house of the Buddha, in India

A-1 Tube and Weldo Valco offers wel-safe tubular products that provide a means of reducing weld-related errors. It is a unique product for use on the inside of tubular components, reducing heat transfer to the weld pool. Using wel-safe tubes makes it possible to weld on the inside surface of the tube and provide full penetration welding without exposing the tube to excessive welding heat or pressure.
All wel-safe tubes have been through an additional manufacturing process by using a patented coating system that provides a non-absorbing, non-carcinogenic zinc phosphate coating and has been found to withstand temperatures up to 2,200°F. This system greatly reduces the potential for burning and weld cracking and provides a smooth and clean weld surface that can be checked by the radiographic process to determine the integrity of the weld and make an assurance of quality. It also allows for inspection of the weld prior to assembly.
Other benefits of wel-safe tubular products are its ability to provide a high degree of mechanical flexibility and strength making it an excellent construction material. Wel-safe tubes are extremely economical and easy to use. All wel-safe products comply with all pertinent standards and are 100% recyclable.The UMass Medical School

As with any country, it's advisable to be cautious and aware of your surroundings.

Eat Your Words! We cook! Inquirers investigate the kitchen, in which recipes are both rules and tools.
Food and cooking are a central part of human life. And yet, the culture of food is mostly invisible. Who is making and selling the food? What are the issues that matter? Eat Your Words! helps you engage in these questions.
Students use cooking to study cultural practices and historical change, from the rise of the supermarket in the 1950s to the influence of celebrity chefs in the past 25 years to the rise of organic foods by and for the elite. Students learn about local food movements in the U.S. and explore what eating healthy means for them in college—or what healthy could mean in college.
And we have the tools to make it work. The curriculum can be used as a series of short units (up to one semester). It includes ready-to-use lesson plans, with full materials for each unit—including handouts, posters, and links to articles, videos, and websites—as well as readings, activities, assignments, and suggestions for assessments.

V.D.’Nemetz Sdn.’: I believe these letters belong on paper. Paper is one of the most undervalued creations in the world. Each sheet of paper, no matter its content, tells the story of a person. But a real story has always fascinated me. It is the one that has yet to be written. And the one that people keep to themselves.
These letters are part of a series of artworks in which stories are not being told, but rather lived. The viewer will get a glimpse of the intimacy and reality of an individual’s life, the things we all wish we could say to our loved ones, especially in our final moments: I love you, I’m sorry, I’m sorry that I hurt you, good luck, I’m proud of you, I’m scared of something, I need your help, I’m dying, I died.
We all carry burdens and secrets inside, and the only way to share them is by speaking them out loud through our own lives. My works use personal and autobiographical elements in order to create a platform for dialogue. To invite each viewer to make a connection with the pieces, and take something home with them from the exhibition.

It might cost nearly nothing to keep kids reading on weekends from kindergarten through fourth, fifth and sixth grade.
But it’s an investment that saves a lot of money down the road, and that keeps kids from doing poorly in other classes.
It’s not a surprise to anyone who’s been around for a while, but reading doesn’t happen only in school.
When a kid can’t read by third grade, it’s often a red flag that the problem will get worse as the child gets older.
In the classroom, when children are unable to read aloud to the class and participate in discussions, it affects the social lives of the children. At home, it tends to create tension, and many kids have trouble adjusting.
Reading is the building block in academics. Many children, especially elementary school children, need to know how to read to learn to write.
And kids who struggle to read by the end of third grade often find the rest of their education difficult.

On 19 Sep 2001 20 May, 2009 By katesausenThe World’s Smallest Airport
The air-conditioned Boeing 747, which was built specifically for the airport
In the middle of a mountain range in Tibet, you will find the world’s smallest airport. The air-conditioned Boeing 747, which was built specifically for the airport, will fit in just three parking bays. It’s really just a grass strip, with no runway lights or signs, and a single, small, wind-sock to indicate the direction of wind.
With a runway of only 1150m long, this is definitely not fit for jet travel, but you can land a small turboprop plane here in good weather. This airport opened in 1985 with the first passenger flight landing on June 29 1989. It replaced the airstrip of the same size that was built for the Tibet-Lhasa air mail route.
The airport is an important transport hub in Tibet, connecting with the rest of the country, and you can take buses and trains connecting to the rest of Tibet, Tibet’s capital Lhasa and the famous railway from Lhasa to Qinghai. It’s also popular with foreign visitors and trekkers who plan to ride

Escape room: where time-pressed individuals solve games – to save themselves
It is a popular game, which you can find at many different entertainment centres and shopping malls: you play with a group of people in a room to solve as many different puzzles as possible within a given time limit. The aim is to successfully solve all the questions and escape the room.
Escape rooms – which is a popular game played with a group of people – have gained in popularity worldwide in the past few years. You enter a room of an entertainment center or shopping center and try to solve riddles in order to get out again and escape. While solving puzzles, you have to follow the instructions given to you by the person who designed the game. The goal is to escape within a certain time, for example in 60 minutes, or 90 minutes – which means you have to solve all the puzzles very quickly. These games have nothing to do with real-life escape rooms, in which people try to escape a realistic-looking room.
Escape games first originated in Japan in 2000, but only became popular in Germany as early as 2013. In the following years, they spread throughout Europe and in the US. They are aimed primarily at young people, and the most successful

The whole thing had the added bonus of the first ever use of the chapel music practice room, so I used what I have learnt in that practice room and put a performance together. The organ was used for the first time as a teaching aid, and we learnt that the chapel doesn't even have a keyboard, it's basically a "manual pipe organ". News: Sonia Bazuniewicz’s “River” to be Published in U.S. This Winter
“River”, published in the April 2013 issue of Exile magazine, will be included in an upcoming anthology, “River Tales of the Saint Lawrence”, to be published by White Pine Press in the United States this winter. The book will be compiled with poems from other writers along the Saint Lawrence River and will cover a wide scope in poetry and prose over the last 100 years.
“River Tales of the Saint Lawrence” will be co-published by the University of Notre Dame Press and Brock University in St. Catharines, Ontario.
Sonia’s poem, entitled “River”, was chosen for this book because it is “inspired by real place and is highly personal to the author, reflecting an intimate, sensual awareness of the waterway’s history and culture.”
The poem will also be reproduced in the Fall 2013 issue of The Antigonish Review (Canadian National Poetry Magazine).

About The Indian News
The Indian News (TIN) was founded in March 1976 by the then Bureau of Immigration and member Gurdial Singh Sidhu in Calgary, Alberta. At that time, the Indian community around Calgary was about 2,000. By October of 1976, the paper had started publication after a few attempts, then Gurdial left town to join his fatherland India.
In October 1976, the newspaper went out to subscribers under the management of Sardar Jagdeo Singh and Mr. Manjit Singh Sahota and continued publication until January 1977. In March 1977, with a strong support by the community, the News came back under Gurdial Singh. That year Gurdial, with the help of a team of community members, started publication of The Indian News.
Gurdial Singh Sidhu (founder & Chairman, Indian News)

Belle Delia Pryors Hathwell Dunaiee, 93 of Waxahachie passed away on Monday, January 11, 2021 in Waxahachie, TX.
A memorial service will be held at a later date.
Belle was born on March 22, 1927 in Marengo, Texas. She married William Dunaiee in 1944. She was a member of First Baptist Church of Waxahachie where she was a member of the choir and served as the pianist. Belle earned a Bachelor’s degree from Wayland College (Now Wayland Baptist University). She taught first grade school for 31 years in the Waxahachie school district. Belle had a strong passion for helping others and was a devoted supporter of the Waxahachie Cancer Society, where she served as a board member, Treasurer and President. She is preceded in death by her husband William Dunaiee and their son Robert David Dunaiee.
She is survived by her son William Andrew Dunaiee of Waxahachie, TX; grandchildren Amanda (Trevor) Mundell of Dallas, TX and William David Dunaiee of Washington D.C.; and great grandchildren

Pizza delivery business goes up like a rock - 1 pizza sold in 7 years
WASHINGTON, Pa. (AP) — One slice at a time, Mike Kuznar served up more than 7,000 slices of pizza during his seven-year tenure at one Washington, Pennsylvania, shop.
And he did it all on his own.
Kuznar spent years building a reputation for the best pizza in town, which led to people stopping in at all hours and asking if the place was open.
"Every time the pizza came, it was someone else that was there," said owner Scott Yoder.
It may sound like it was easy. But the Washington Pizza Shop, which had been in business for 40 years until it closed in 2006, didn't have much of a delivery business. And in that time, Kuznar became known as the guy with the best pizza in the area.
"He was one of the hardest-working pizzas we ever had, and just a great guy to work for," Yoder said. "People knew him by name that weren't even customers."
It was Kuznar's first job out of high school, and as he grew out of pizza-boy status, his reputation grew.

Bolingar is another popular local term but you are much luck you will likely pronounce them correctly unless you know that they are pronounced Bolingar. Some other local terms are: (1) to be sick, to feel sick – to have the 'heath' (2) the sun, sun (3) to be well – to have the 'heath' (4) to have fever, fever (5) to be very very cold, cold (6) to stay there, to reside – to reside at Bolingar.

History
The first settlers to the town were the British from the United Kingdom, and its local government was a Municipality.  It was administered by a President.  The municipality was later elevated and changed to a Sub-Divisional Officer.  The government of a Sub-Divisional Officer in Bolingar was changed to an Administrative Officer.

Vintage Fashion Collection at Art Nail and Salewww,
by Doree Armor
We've all been there. You're wearing jeans so high you might as well be wearing jeans with tights, and suddenly, there's a need to put on an article of clothing that has a lot going on so you look interesting, not, well, like you're wearing pants with tights. But instead of a pair of vintage pants with a pair of high-ankle boots that may or may not have pockets, you put on a pair of vintage pumps. The problem? You look like you're going to a church picnic, especially since the tops of the pumps are showing more than the bottoms. Brighten the Corner with Light
by Jonathan Cohn, Senior Fellow at the Van Alen Institute
A vacant lot along the East/West Highway has, until recently, been a dark and dangerous place. It was also a place where residents of the Harlem neighborhood would encounter police and other authorities with less than positive intent. In order to prevent such encounters, the Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA) has installed a security light, designed with input from local residents and safety experts.
In the first week, the local police reported a significant reduction in complaints from residents. The light, which is on 24/7 and is a fixture designed to be hard to climb, is also not visible from the roadway, making it less likely to invite trouble for pedestrians and cyclists. There have been no reports of vandalism.
At the launch, MTA Chairman Jay Walder noted that the agency was able to secure some federal funding, which helped make the light possible.
The light, which was fabricated by the Lighting Research Group at Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute

Pell, former Archbishop of Sydney who now is the first Australian cardinal in the College of Cardinals, gave opening remarks Aug. 30 at the summer meeting of the Italian bishops’ conference in Rimini.

"It is only in governance that this culture of accountability can be embedded in every aspect of the workings of the church," he said. As a curiosity for people like me, who are fascinated by old people of the past and wish we had access to a whole bunch of their stuff, it is more than worth a look. If Your Partner Has An AddiCup - Are Women Being Equitible Equipped to Perform Themselves Under Pressure?
At the end of last year, UK scientists were making headlines following their experiment with adding the Addison's Cups (fertility drugs) to the women's morning coffee. This gave them a boost in the morning just before they went to work, in order to prevent their body crashing. This has been the case for countless women over the past 30 years, but the experiment was new; it was the first time that scientists had put all the women through the drug-testing before and after the trial. The study found that the morning coffee actually brought about a decline in fertility within 3 months; it can increase the risk of the woman miscarrying, as well as making the ovaries more susceptible to cysts.
This was the first study in the past 30 years and so the results were quite dramatic. The doctors responsible for the study found this rather 'unfortunate' as if such information were available, it would allow the women to be made aware of the risk, and therefore they could take a decision to either take the risk or abstain - but there was no real information available to the women.

On 23 April 2013 an Agave paraguariensis in Jardem Natural from Rio de Janeiro was killed by the fungal disease known as 'Diatom-itis'.
The plant was about 20 years old and had grown to over 1.5 m tall and its leaves reached a height of 20 cm and width of 10 cm.
The symptoms of the disease started with black growths on the leaves that spread like spots.
The spots were in rows like those caused by Xanthomonas.
The black spots soon developed into long white strips similar to those caused by 'Bunt' (Pompholyx cornutus).
In the beginning the growth of the disease was slow but in the last 10 days it increased exponentially.
We have not yet found any explanation, but one would at first think: The disease is very rapid in places with humidity around or above 70 %, even higher in the case of 'bunt'.
A good idea to prevent the disease is to remove the dying leaves and to spray the plant with copper, preferably with copper sulfate.
If this method fails the disease cannot be avoided.

EGGLINTON TO BOLTON
On 29th December 1839 at the request of John Bryden, the vicar of Oundle, the Revd. Robert Wilmot made a survey and valuation of the estate of Mr. Bryden in the parishes of Oundle, Burton-d'Eath, Upton, Weldon, Kites-hardwick, Stanton, &c. The result was the making of a book of reference giving the estate and lands that were then in Bryden's name in each parish. The survey of the estate covered 16,500 acres - it was in poor and dilapidated condition.
The Bryden family had lived in Oundle, on and off, for many generations. John Bryden's paternal grandfather, William, had built Bryden's Castle, now a ruin, a large stone house on the road to Weldon.
John Bryden's parents, John of Bignold and Mary Bury, daughter of Thomas Bury, of High-Bignold, and his wife, Margaret

St. George and University Hospitals Birmingham NHS Trust is celebrating its 100,000 members of staff through the NHS staff card.
The card is a nationally recognised digital staff identity that allows staff to access a wide range of NHS services, pay for services, and receive digital discounts as well as supporting NHS staff to work in and across Trusts.
The card will bring convenience into staff members’ lives by providing a quick and easy way to pay at a cash machine, use public transport, claim discounts and access a wide range of local services.

This has brought together the different parts of the hospital, recognising all our roles and the importance that we all play in the work we do.
I really want to commend the initiative and the team that have made this happen. Staff are at the heart of everything we do, it’s very uplifting.

Habenarias found commonly distributed in Southern United Sattings are given as Toxic (HAB) and Toxic (TSRC). HAB indicates that a toxin is believed to have a harmful effect on fish, invertebrates and birds. Therefore most species in that category are toxic. Tropical Sagebrush can be toxic and a potential human health hazard and is listed as Toxic (TSRC) and a contact poison.
The HAB is indicated by the number 1 and TSRC indicated by the number 2.
The HAB has the following toxin content or toxicity:
Toxicity: The total amount of toxin (toxin is made up of a group of different chemicals) from plant.
0.10 mg/g: Not toxic or HAB
0.50 mg/g: Moderately toxic
1.0 mg/g: Potentially toxic
1.50 mg/g: Extremely toxic
The TSRC has the following toxin content or toxicity:
1.0 mg/g: Potentially toxic

Obit: David Edward Jones (“Pike,” died September 2014 )... ( Ormiston )...
David Edward Jones, age 78, of Ormiston, passed away Sunday September 14, 2014 surrounded by his loving family.
Born in Duncannon on January 29, 1936 he was the son of the late Harry and Edith (Stern) Jones
He was a loving husband, father, grandfather, great-grandfather, brother, son, uncle and lifelong friend. One of his proudest achievements, he was a veteran of the United States Air Force, where he proudly served his country and served on an aircraft carrier during the Korean War. He was also a faithful and active member of Mt. Carmel United Church of Christ, the H. Lee Allen Post American Legion, and was a member of the Ormiston Volunteer Fire Department for many years.
In addition to his parents he is preceded in death by a sister, June Jones.

My mom (Guset Yulita Erenado Estradas De Los Reyes) who is 91 years old is still in good shape but is suffering from Alzheimer’s disease.
In an Alzheimer’s sufferer’s family, his loved one is not the one suffering. They are. But their minds are not in the present at all, not able to recall anything that transpired in the past, much less the present. The family members are still there for their loved one, still there beside him, but they, too are suffering and are often helpless not being able to do anything to help their loved one.
My mother has been having hallucinations that her grandchildren are trying to kill her. She knows they are not, but still her subconscious believes this and so she is scared of her own children and grandchildren. I don’t know how she has this hallucination. Maybe one of my brothers has told her their children have done things to her when they were growing up. Maybe she is seeing things, not seeing things at all.
No wonder she’s scared, living in a state of hallucination.

This course discusses important current policy regarding women-in the news on all platforms and ways individuals and groups can advocate for positive change.
Newsmakers often report the challenges of balancing family and work. They often also cover important policy issues to do with workplace discrimination, childcare and work-family balance in the media.
Courses will discuss the need for women/career journalists, to provide a forum that will allow women/career journalists to report work/family balance, which may or may not pertain to their work.
Participants should take home useful information and skills to apply to their journalism practice.
Course co-ordinator (c) Dr. Yvonne Rydin (yvonnerydin@gmail.com) and the Women In Radio Committee.

The second day will bring a more focused examination of the topics with practical application.
There will be 3 courses.

It seemed to hit fast as many around them started crying from seeing the incident happened; a small girl named Alena’s face changed completely as she began to lose a lot of blood, but not too soon to notice, she had a big scar right below her eye that was still bleeding.
“Where is that big ugly face?” a boy cried while walking out of the crowd watching Alena.
The boy suddenly had a big grin on his face as he spotted the face he was looking for; he could not remember his name, but all he knew was that his face was pretty famous, for many people believed that he was the only one that could do whatever he wanted.
The boy walked towards him and began to strike his head again on his face.
“Who’s that big ugly face here?” He asked again.
“You’ll see” Alena replied without even looking at him.
With a huge grin on his face, the boy began to move away from Alena and walked towards the crowd.
“I believe she knows who that big ugly face is” said a boy who tried to calm down the crowd who was still making noise.
Alena looked up at the boy with the huge grin and laughed.

The second part (pp. 11-16) consists of several photographs showing the same group of people, all in their kitchens. The photos show how the women in this group prepared the food they had just prepared in a more elaborate way. Some of the recipes in the first part of the article (pp. 1-7, 8-15) are not illustrated. Founded as The Pipken P.S.O., is now named Leland Legacy. Purchased in 2007, Leland Legacy was originally known as The Pipken P.S.O. The purpose of The Pipken P.S.O. was to promote positive social interaction among the young adults living on the eastside of Jackson, Mississippi. During the late 1990’s “downtown” Jackson became the site of several residential housing developments where many young adults were relocated from other parts of the state as well as the country. This change created a need for a social group that would bring the young adult population together, create awareness of the different opportunities for a positive future and provide the young adults with a means of social interaction to improve self-esteem within the community and beyond.
Founded in 1998, The Pipken P.S.O. took its name from the famous Pipken House on the University of Mississippi campus. The Pipken House was a place where friends could meet up on campus after class. Pho (ฏ pronounced phon) refers in Thai to both a traditional type of seafood soup popular on the Thai archipelago which dates back to King Taksin and is served chilled, an easy drinking sour soup, a variety of seafood soups served in coconut milk with or without Thai sweet basil or chili chili dips with a wide assortment of meat, vegetables and seasonings.
Pho is a famous dish of Northern Vietnam and has been described as a dish of humble origins. Many of the ingredients used are found in the markets of Hanoi, and are sold in the alleys by street vendors called "pho bo" or "pho ba" or as street food vendors. In most large cities across Northern Vietnam they can be found. The "pho bo" or "pho ba" is either on a cart or a table.
The Vietnamese often eat the same food at home as they do when dining out. Pho is so common that it is taken for granted, so it is served at many restaurants. Usually, it is a bowl of soup with noodles and is often accompanied by a basket of fresh herbs and vegetables, including sugar cane and other ingredients.
Traditionally, Pho was only eaten during the warm summer months. When

I saw three dips in five meters of swimming last September and didn‘ d go in at any of them—I still had a couple years left in me that’s for sure! I was also in the ocean all alone (alone, not scared alone, I don’t scare that easy) and no one around. The surf was nice about shoulder high.
I swam out to the biggest swell I’d seen in a while. I was in water waist deep and had to swim. It‘ s like something inside you tells you to go and you start moving, without even thinking about it—a kind of intuition you have. After a minute or two I took a breath. I looked behind me and saw about fifteen seals in the sea, playing in the swells. I looked up the crests to see a humpback whale surfacing.
I could see his fluke, his dorsal fin and all his other features. He was only about thirty feet away from me, and I thought my heart would have to beat out of my chest.
I wanted to go over there and tell him that he‘ s very graceful and to keep it up—I also wanted to tell him to keep watching, to have a good eye on

How Far can You Travel without Flying with BTS BTS Fanart
Mikala Hester
A series of BTS fanart that is part of a larger project aimed at promoting intercity travel.
This project is a collaboration with Hye-Yun Oh (KU) and Shunsuke Suto (KYOTO TECH).
It is a series of animations that aim to explore the role of BTS within the cultural and social landscapes of South Korea, the US, and Japan. These short animations are inspired by the fan-based activities of K-pop fandoms and use a hybrid of cut paper animation, CGI, and photo processing to highlight the complexity of South Korean cultural and social dynamics as well as the way in which fans interpret, contextualize, and consume media produced within and outside of Korea.
These animations were produced over a span of several months during the winter of 2018 and early spring of 2019 and focus on four case studies that highlight the social dynamics between South Korea, Japan, and the US through the relationship of BTS and fandom.
The four animations, along with the larger project, will be presented and discussed as a collaboration between the US, South Korea, and Japan at IAMAS 2019

Hans Zatz
Trained within what later the Austrian avant­-garde identified as “Actionisme”, Hans Zatz’s “Actionists” paintings have since the early 1960s been associated with “New Vienna Secession” and with Art & Language, the Anglo-American artist collective also inspired and influenced both by “Actionism.” The works are based on the systematic analysis of the human body, using only white chalk, to investigate the physical expression of emotion. Zatz’s works are to be understood as visual narratives, in some instances with accompanying audio components, dealing with sexual taboos as a social phenomenon. With a minimalistic approach he attempts to define the physical parameters of love-making; that is, not without success.
Hans Zatz, born in Vienna, 1946, lives and works in Vienna.

What is Diatomaceous Earth
DIATOMACEOUS EARTH (DE) is finely ground fossilized, microscopic aquatic plant remains, commonly called diatoms. This is a non-toxic (not a chemical) and 100% organic substance. DE can be used internally and externally as a deodorant, insect repellent, water softener, dust control, deodorizer, and as a natural pesticide/insecticide.
Radiation Chelation Therapy Information
Radiation Chelation is a natural process that occurs when the body breaks down radioactive particles and eliminates them from the body. It is a process by which the body uses antioxidants like Vitamins C and E, to eliminate the radioactive particles. New ‘Sharbat Jayate Charkhi Chirkhel Song Torn in Pakistan
Asha Bhosle’s newest song, Sharbat Jayate Charkhi Chirkhel, has got embroiled in another controversy in Pakistan. This is the second time this song from the upcoming Bollywood film, ‘Love Aaj Kal’, is in controversy, having been banned a few weeks back for alleged ‘objectionable’ lyrics.
The song, which is a duet sung by Asha and singer Anu Malik, is being viewed as derogatory by Pakistani groups. It is alleged that the song compares the women of Pakistan to prostitutes and refers to the female lead of the film as a ‘dowry-giver’.
Asha is expected to fly to Pakistan again this month to perform at the Pakistani premiere of the film. However, her invitation is unlikely to be accepted by the Pakistani government because of her latest controversy.
Watch the video here:
Indian women are often called sex-workers in Pakistan because of the dowry system in place. Asha’s song is not the first in Bollywood that has created controversy.
In 2002, the title song for the film ‘Saathiya’ was banned

By stimulating the entire body in a non-painful manner the InfraStim® System allows the body to create its own natural warmth. This can be up to eight times more effective than other forms of temperature.
InfraStim® is currently undergoing regulatory review for the FDA Class II and CE classification.
